layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid typical of WordPress themes, with content max-width likely around 1140–1200px inside full-width sections.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear top-to-bottom structure consistent with the mockup: a full-width dark header bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right; directly beneath it, a full-width hero section with the opportunity summary text overlaid on a photographic background, positioned slightly left-of-center and in the lower third of the hero. Below the hero, the layout transitions into a two-column main content area constrained to a ~1140–1200px max-width: a wider main column on the left for narrative detail and shift listing, and a narrower right sidebar for key actions (registration form, contact/location) and status feedback, echoing the mockup’s main-content-plus-sidebar pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear top-to-bottom structure consistent with the mockup: a full-width dark header bar with logo on the left and navigation on the right; directly beneath it, a full-width hero section with the opportunity summary text overlaid on a photographic background, positioned slightly left-of-center and in the lower third of the hero. Below the hero, the layout transitions into a two-column main content area constrained to a ~1140–1200px max-width: a wider main column on the left for narrative detail and shift listing, and a narrower right sidebar for key actions (registration form, contact/location) and status feedback, echoing the mockup’s main-content-plus-sidebar pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the dark navigation bar occupies roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height, the hero image and its overlaid opportunity summary occupy about 45–55%, and the remaining 25–40% reveals the top of the two-column content area. Horizontally, the header reserves about 30% for the logo and 70% for navigation links. In the main area, the left content column uses about 65–70% of the width (8 of 12 grid columns) and the right sidebar uses 30–35% (4 of 12 columns). Section spacing follows the 8px-based scale: 48–64px between major sections (hero to main content; description to shifts) and 16–24px between related elements within a section.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is moderate: the hero is visually rich but text-light, with a single prominent headline and a compact status indicator to maintain a clean, editorial feel. The main content column supports more text (description, shift list) but maintains legibility via clear headings, 16–24px vertical separation, and consistent left alignment. The right sidebar is functionally denser, clustering the registration form, status messages, and contact/location into stacked, well-padded blocks, but each block retains comfortable padding (12–16px vertical, 16–24px horizontal) and clear separation to avoid visual crowding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F- then Z-pattern similar to the mockup. The eye begins at the top-left logo, sweeps right through the navigation, then drops diagonally into the hero where the main opportunity title in white text over the photo becomes the primary focal point. From the title and its underline/accent, attention is pulled slightly right to the availability indicator. As the user scrolls, the flow continues into the main content left column (description, then shift list) and then across to the right sidebar, where the registration form and status feedback create a strong secondary interaction focal point in the lower-right region of the content area, analogous to the WhatsApp button and search in the mockup.

layout.overall_description: The Volunteer Opportunity Detail page presents as a modern, high-contrast WordPress-style layout: a full-width black header with the initiative’s wordmark on the left and neatly spaced navigation links on the right sits at the very top. Beneath it stretches a full-width hero image related to the outreach work, dimmed slightly to allow a bold white opportunity title to sit over it, placed a bit left of center and in the lower third of the image, with a crisp accent line under the headline. A subtle back-to-list link appears in the top-left of this hero content band, while a compact availability pill tucks to the right of the title, balancing the composition. As the hero’s lower edge is reached, the layout snaps into a centered two-column grid: a broad left column for the detailed description and a stacked list of upcoming shifts, and a narrower right sidebar that collects the registration form, status messages, and practical location/contact information. Generous 48–64px vertical gaps clearly separate the hero from the main content and the major sections from each other, while 16–24px padding inside each card-like block keeps the design airy but deliberate. The overall effect mirrors a contemporary nonprofit landing page: editorial and image-driven at the top, then progressively more structured and task-oriented as the eye moves down toward the right-side registration area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual controller spans the logical bounds of the entire page, conceptually sitting behind all visible components. It does not occupy explicit space in the layout under normal conditions. When global page states require it (for example, a fatal load error), it can trigger a full-page overlay that covers the area from just below the primary navigation bar to the bottom of the viewport, with a semi-transparent dark scrim and a centered message or retry button, temporarily dimming the hero, main content, and sidebar while preserving the header’s visibility.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normally non-rendering in the visual layout; when activated for global errors, it behaves like a fixed overlay covering the content area beneath the header, centered both vertically and horizontally within the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary navigation bar is a full-width, black horizontal bar anchored to the very top of the page. It spans 100% of the viewport width and sits above all other components. Within a centered 1140–1200px max-width container, the logo/wordmark block occupies roughly the left 30% of the horizontal space, left-aligned with the grid. The navigation links are right-aligned in the remaining 70%, arranged in a single horizontal row with generous 24px gaps, following the mockup’s spacious header feel. Vertical height is moderate but substantial (about 64–80px including padding), with 12–16px top and bottom padding around the logo and nav links to create a solid, high-contrast band that sets the tone of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Stays pinned to the top edge of the page; it may be configured as sticky so that it remains visible at the top when the user scrolls, with the rest of the page content (hero and below) scrolling underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The opportunity-detail-loader occupies the same horizontal bounds as the main content stack (from the top of the hero section down to the bottom of the page content). When active during initial data loading, it overlays the hero and the two-column area below with a slightly translucent dark scrim, leaving the navigation bar visible above. A circular spinner or loading indicator is centered horizontally and vertically within the visible viewport area of the content region, giving the impression that the entire hero and content column area is temporarily suspended. Once loading completes, the overlay disappears and underlying components (hero summary, description, shifts, registration form) become interactive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute/fixed overlay relative to the content area below the navigation bar, covering the hero and main content while active; otherwise occupies no layout space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The opportunity summary forms the core of the hero section immediately below the primary navigation bar. A full-width hero image stretches edge-to-edge across the viewport, with its height set to roughly 45–55% of the viewport height on large screens. A subtle dark gradient overlay from bottom to top slightly mutes the lower portion of the image. Within this hero, a centered content container (aligned to the same 1140–1200px max-width grid as the header) holds the summary text. The main opportunity title sits slightly left of the center line horizontally and positioned in the lower third of the hero’s height, set in large white typography. Directly under the title, a short, colored accent line or underline runs about one-third the width of the title, reinforcing the editorial feel. The back-to-list link is visually associated with this component by sitting in the same content container, but near the top-left of the hero: a small text link approximately 16–24px below the bottom edge of the navigation bar and aligned with the left boundary of the title. On the right side of the title’s baseline, the availability indicator appears as a pill or badge, creating a compact horizontal grouping of title + status that stands out against the image backdrop. Any brief meta information (e.g., category or date) can appear as a smaller line of text above or below the main title with 8–12px spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow, directly under the navigation bar; the hero height adjusts responsively, reducing on smaller screens, while the text block remains vertically centered in the lower third of the image for a consistent focal point.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The description section is the first block in the left main content column beneath the hero. It sits in a centered max-width container that splits into two columns: this section occupies roughly 65–70% of the width (about 8 of 12 grid columns) on the left. There is a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px between the bottom edge of the hero and the top of this section, visually signaling the transition from editorial to informational content. Inside the section, a heading such as “About this opportunity” is left-aligned and placed at the top, with 16–24px of space below it before the descriptive paragraphs begin. Text lines span a comfortable 60–75 characters per line. The block runs downwards for as many paragraphs as needed, with 16px spacing between paragraphs, and ends with a 32–40px gap before the next major component (the shift list) in this left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally as the topmost element in the left column beneath the hero, expanding vertically as needed; on smaller screens, it becomes full-width above other content, maintaining generous top and bottom margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The location and contact section lives in the right sidebar column, aligning visually with the top of the left column’s description section. Within the centered page container, it occupies about 30–35% of the width (roughly 4 of 12 grid columns) on the right. At desktop widths, it appears as a compact card-like block with a subtle border or shadow, 16–24px internal padding, and 24px of separation from any components stacked above or below it in the sidebar. A concise heading such as “Location & Contact” sits at the top, with address, service times, or contact details listed below in a vertical arrangement with 8–12px spacing. If a small map thumbnail is present, it sits below the text, filling the card’s width with 8–16px padding. Vertically, this block occupies the upper region of the sidebar, either directly above or just below the registration form depending on priority; when the registration form is prioritized, this section sits immediately underneath it with 24–32px space separating the two blocks.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar column, stacked with other sidebar components; on small screens it collapses into the single-column flow, appearing below the registration form but still clearly separated from adjacent sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shift list occupies the main left column beneath the opportunity description. It spans the same 65–70% width as the description (about 8 grid columns) and begins approximately 32–40px below the end of the description text, maintaining the moderate vertical rhythm. A section heading like “Upcoming Shifts” is left-aligned at the top, with 16–24px space before the first shift item. Each shift appears as a horizontally full card or row within this column, with 12–16px vertical padding, 16–24px horizontal padding, and 12–16px vertical gaps between items. The list can extend downward beyond the initial viewport; the page scrolls normally. On long lists, the right sidebar with the registration form visually anchors the right side, while the shift items on the left create a vertical stack that the eye scans from top to bottom. The bottom of the list leaves a 48–64px margin before the page footer or any subsequent sections, preserving the airy feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, expanding vertically as additional shifts are loaded; on small screens, it becomes full-width and sits below the description and above the registration form, maintaining consistent spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there are no available shifts, the empty-state banner appears in exactly the same position and width as the shift list in the left column, directly beneath the description section. It spans about 65–70% of the page width, aligned with the left content column. The banner is styled as a full-width card with a soft, tinted background and 16–24px internal padding, with its top positioned 32–40px below the end of the description text. A concise message such as “No upcoming shifts are currently available for this opportunity” is centered or left-aligned within the banner, with optional secondary guidance text below in smaller type. The overall height is compact, visually indicating a lack of items without overwhelming the page. There remains 48–64px of white space below this banner before any following content or footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same static slot as the shift list within the left column and replaces it when there are no shifts; it does not overlay or float, and it collapses the vertical space that would otherwise be used by shift items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shift registration form is the primary actionable block in the right sidebar, mirroring the prominence of the search and floating action area from the mockup. It occupies the full width of the sidebar column (about 30–35% of the page, roughly 4 grid columns) and is placed near the top of the sidebar, aligning vertically with either the upper portion of the shift list or slightly above it to emphasize its importance. The form is housed in a card with a subtle border or shadow, 16–24px padding on all sides, and 24–32px separation from neighboring sidebar blocks like the location/contact section. Input fields are stacked vertically with 12–16px spacing, and the primary submit button sits at the bottom, spanning most of the card’s width. A modest 16–24px margin at the bottom of the card separates it from the next sidebar component. Visually, this side column card sits roughly in the lower-right quadrant of the initial viewport as the user begins to scroll past the hero, naturally drawing attention similar to the WhatsApp button in the original design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar column, but functionally central; on smaller screens, it flows into the single-column layout and is placed directly beneath the shift list (or empty-state banner), maintaining strong visual emphasis through card styling and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration status message appears as a feedback banner directly associated with the registration form. It shares the same width as the form card in the right sidebar and is positioned immediately above the form, with a tight 8–12px gap to clearly link the two components. The banner uses a colored background (green for success, red/orange for errors, neutral for info) with 12–16px padding and slightly rounded corners, but is visually lighter than the form card so it doesn’t overpower it. When active, it nudges the form slightly downward in the sidebar, increasing the vertical stack height but maintaining the same right alignment. If no status needs to be shown, this banner collapses entirely, allowing the form to rise closer to the top of the sidebar. On mobile, it appears full-width directly above the form in the single-column flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the right sidebar’s vertical flow, occupying space only when there is a message to show; it does not overlay the form and simply pushes surrounding content up or down as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state-updater component has no direct visual footprint but conceptually operates in the same horizontal band as the shift list and registration form. It listens to shifts and registration actions and updates the availability information—such as spots remaining in the shift list and the availability indicator in the hero. When it triggers changes, small, localized updates occur: for instance, the number of remaining spots in each shift row updates in place, and the pill in the hero may shift from “Open” to “Full,” without any new dedicated UI area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and embedded in the page’s logic layer; it does not occupy layout space but drives dynamic text and status changes in existing visible components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability indicator is a compact status pill integrated into the hero’s opportunity summary area. Within the hero’s content container, it is positioned to the right of or slightly below the main title, aligned along the title’s baseline or just beneath its right edge. It occupies a small fraction of the hero width—roughly the width of a short word like “Open” plus 16–24px horizontal padding—and stands out with a solid background color (e.g., green for available, gray for full) and white or dark text, depending on contrast needs. There is an 8–12px gap between the edge of the title text and the pill. This positioning ensures that as the user’s eye moves from the title, it naturally encounters the status indicator as part of the same visual unit. On smaller screens, the pill shifts under the title in a stacked arrangement but remains right-aligned or center-left within the text block, maintaining proximity and emphasis.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero content block, anchored relative to the opportunity title; it updates its label and color dynamically based on underlying data but does not move or overlay other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-list link appears in the upper-left portion of the hero area, clearly visible but visually lightweight. It is placed inside the same max-width hero content container as the title, aligned with the hero’s left grid margin. Vertically, it sits near the top of the hero image—about 16–24px below the bottom edge of the navigation bar—creating a small horizontal row with a left-pointing icon or chevron followed by the link text (e.g., “Back to opportunities”). There is a comfortable 8–12px gap between this link and any meta text or the main title below, so the link does not compete with the headline. On scroll, it scrolls away with the hero content. On smaller screens, the link remains pinned to the top-left of the hero content block, sometimes stacking directly above the title, but always aligned to the left margin, giving users an immediate, intuitive escape route back to the listing page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero content, part of the normal document flow; it does not stick to the viewport and scrolls away when the user moves past the hero.

name: Events
title: Events
description: Search, filter, and browse upcoming church events, classes, outreach opportunities, and neighborhood cleanups, with links to detailed event pages.

core_features: Load primary navigation items for the site header using getPrimaryNavigation.
Load dynamic filter and sort options for the events listing using getEventFilterOptions.
Initialize all filter and sort controls from URL query parameters when the page loads.
Execute event searches using searchEvents with the current filter and sort state.
Display a list of events from searchEvents including basic details (title, date/time, campus/location, cost, format, activity level, outreach eligibility, and status).
Allow users to refine results by category, cost, format, campus, location (ZIP and radius), date range, day of week, time of day, activity level, and outreach eligibility.
Allow users to change sorting (e.g., by date or other available sort options) and re-run searchEvents.
Allow users to click an event card or a details link to navigate to the event detail page with the eventId in the URL.
Keep the URL query parameters in sync with the current filter and sort state so results can be shared and revisited.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Load Events page directly via navigation
1. User clicks the 'Events' item in the primary navigation.
2. Browser navigates to events.html without query parameters.
3. Page calls getPrimaryNavigation to render the header menu.
4. Page calls getEventFilterOptions to load dynamic filter and sort options.
5. URL is parsed; no relevant query parameters are found, so default filter state is created (no specific filters, default sort based on the first option from getEventFilterOptions.sortOptions if available).
6. Page calls searchEvents with all arguments derived from this default state (typically empty or null values for filters and a default sort key if defined).
7. The returned list from searchEvents is rendered as event cards in the results list.
8. User can scroll through events and click any event card or 'View details' link to go to event_detail.html?eventId={eventId}.

Workflow 2: Arrive with pre-selected category (e.g., from 'Neighborhood cleanups' navigation link)
1. User clicks a link such as 'Neighborhood cleanups' in the header or content area.
2. Browser navigates to events.html with a categoryId query parameter.
3. Page calls getPrimaryNavigation and getEventFilterOptions.
4. URL query parameters are parsed; the categoryId value is stored as the initial category filter state.
5. Filter controls are initialized so that the filter corresponding to that categoryId is pre-selected.
6. Page calls searchEvents, passing the categoryId value as the categoryKey parameter and using other filter parameters from the URL or defaults if absent.
7. Results list shows only events in that category.
8. User may adjust or clear the category filter; when changed, the URL is updated and searchEvents is called again with the new categoryKey value.

Workflow 3: Use advanced filters (location, date, time, activity level, outreach eligible)
1. User lands on events.html, either directly or with some pre-populated parameters (e.g., zip, radiusMiles, dateRangeStart, dateRangeEnd, dayOfWeek, timeOfDay, activityLevelKey, isOutreachEligibleOnly).
2. Page loads navigation and filter options via getPrimaryNavigation and getEventFilterOptions.
3. URL parameters are read; corresponding filter controls are pre-populated (e.g., ZIP field, radius selector, date range picker, day-of-week selector, time-of-day selector, activity level selector, outreach-eligible toggle).
4. Page calls searchEvents using all current filter states as arguments.
5. User refines filters, for example:
   - Enters or edits ZIP and radius.
   - Adjusts start/end dates.
   - Selects a specific day of week and time of day.
   - Selects an activity level.
   - Toggles outreach-only events.
6. On each change (or on an explicit 'Apply filters' action), the page updates the relevant query parameters in the URL and calls searchEvents again with the updated arguments.
7. The results list updates to reflect the new criteria.
8. User can clear all filters with a reset action, which removes related query parameters, resets filter controls, and re-runs searchEvents with cleared filters.

Workflow 4: Sort and share results
1. User lands on events.html and applies a combination of filters.
2. User changes the sort control to one of the options returned by getEventFilterOptions.sortOptions.
3. Page updates the sortBy query parameter in the URL and calls searchEvents with the selected sort key.
4. Results list is re-rendered in the new order.
5. User copies the URL from the browser and shares it.
6. Another user opens the shared URL; the page reads all query parameters (including sortBy), initializes filter/sort controls to match, and calls searchEvents with those values, reproducing the same result set and ordering.

Workflow 5: Navigate to event detail
1. From the results list, user identifies an event of interest.
2. User clicks anywhere on the event card designated as interactive, or on a 'View details' link within the card.
3. Browser navigates to event_detail.html with the event's identifier in the query string, e.g., event_detail.html?eventId={eventId}.
4. Event detail page (not defined here) can then use eventId to load the full details and enable registration or RSVP.
interactions: Header navigation click: Clicking a primary nav item (e.g., 'Events', 'Serve', 'Give') triggers standard navigation to the corresponding page URL. For 'Events', it navigates to events.html with or without additional query parameters as defined by the link. For dedicated category links (e.g., a link that includes categoryId), the browser navigates to events.html with that parameter set.

Filter control change (category, cost, format, campus): When the user changes any of these controls, the page updates the internal filter state, synchronizes the corresponding URL parameter (or removes it if cleared), and then calls searchEvents with updated arguments. Once results return, the event list component is re-rendered.

Location filter change (ZIP and radius): When the user enters or updates a ZIP code or radius, the zip and radiusMiles URL parameters are updated. Depending on the designed UX, searchEvents may be called immediately on change or after an explicit 'Apply filters' action. The updated zip and radiusMiles values are passed to searchEvents.

Date range filter change: Updating the start or end date updates dateRangeStart and/or dateRangeEnd in the URL. After the change, searchEvents is called with these new values to constrain events by date.
Day of week and time of day selection: Selecting or clearing these filters updates dayOfWeek and timeOfDay in the URL and triggers a new call to searchEvents.
Activity level selection: Changing this control updates activityLevelKey in the URL and triggers a new call to searchEvents, refining especially neighborhood cleanup events by activity level.

Outreach-eligible toggle: Toggling this control updates isOutreachEligibleOnly in the URL (typically to a boolean representation consistent with the backend’s expectations). The page calls searchEvents with the new isOutreachEligibleOnly value so that only events that can be added to an outreach plan are included when true.

Sort selection change: When the user selects a different sort option, sortBy is updated in the URL, and searchEvents is called again with that sort key, reordering the existing result set based on backend sorting.

Reset/clear filters: Activating a 'Clear all filters' interaction removes all filter-related query parameters from the URL (categoryId, campusId, zip, radiusMiles, dateRangeStart, dateRangeEnd, dayOfWeek, timeOfDay, activityLevelKey, isOutreachEligibleOnly, and optionally sortBy if resetting to default). Filter controls revert to their default values, and searchEvents is called with cleared filter arguments.
Event card click: Clicking on an event card or its 'View details' link initiates navigation to event_detail.html with eventId set to that event's identifier from searchEvents.

Initial page load: On first render, the page parses the current URL’s query parameters and sets the internal state for each corresponding filter. After filter options are available from getEventFilterOptions, searchEvents is called once using these states so the initial event list reflects any incoming pre-filtering.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Events page treats the URL query string as the single source of truth for the current filter and sort state. On page load, it parses the following parameters: categoryId, campusId, zip, radiusMiles, dateRangeStart, dateRangeEnd, dayOfWeek, timeOfDay, activityLevelKey, isOutreachEligibleOnly, and sortBy.

Mapping URL parameters to searchEvents arguments:
- categoryId (URL) maps directly to the categoryKey argument in searchEvents.
- campusId (URL) maps directly to the campusId argument in searchEvents.
- zip (URL) maps directly to the zip argument in searchEvents.
- radiusMiles (URL) maps directly to the radiusMiles argument in searchEvents.
- dateRangeStart (URL) maps directly to the dateRangeStart argument in searchEvents.
- dateRangeEnd (URL) maps directly to the dateRangeEnd argument in searchEvents.
- dayOfWeek (URL) maps directly to the dayOfWeek argument in searchEvents.
- timeOfDay (URL) maps directly to the timeOfDay argument in searchEvents.
- activityLevelKey (URL) maps directly to the activityLevelKey argument in searchEvents.
- isOutreachEligibleOnly (URL) maps directly to the isOutreachEligibleOnly argument in searchEvents. The page is responsible for converting between URL-safe representation and the boolean type expected by searchEvents.
- sortBy (URL) maps directly to the sortBy argument in searchEvents.

Cost and format filters do not have explicit incoming URL parameters defined. If the implementation needs to persist these states, it may introduce additional URL parameters (e.g., for costFilter and formatKey) and ensure they are passed as the costFilter and formatKey arguments in searchEvents. Any such parameters must be synchronized both ways: reading on load to initialize control state and writing on user changes.

Initialization flow:
1. On load, the page calls getEventFilterOptions without parameters.
2. Once filter options are available, the page reads the current URL and constructs an internal filter state object where each property corresponds to one of the searchEvents parameters.
3. For each URL parameter present, the internal state’s property is set to that value. For parameters not present, the state is set to a neutral value (such as null or an equivalent) or, for sortBy, potentially a default derived from getEventFilterOptions.sortOptions.
4. The page calls searchEvents with all these state values.

Ongoing state updates:
- Any user interaction that changes filters or sorting updates the internal filter state first, then updates the URL query parameters to match that state. Only after the URL is synchronized is searchEvents called again with the updated state.
- If the browser back/forward buttons are used and the URL query string changes, the page listens for those changes, re-parses the URL into filter state, updates UI controls, and re-invokes searchEvents so the results reflect the historical state.

No localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used. All persisted state between page loads and navigations is encoded in the URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid typical of WordPress themes, with content max-width likely around 1140–1200px inside full-width sections.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a top-to-bottom, full-width structure that mirrors the mockup: (1) a dark full-width header bar containing the primary navigation; (2) a full-width hero section for the Events page with a photograph and overlaid headline; (3) below the hero, a centered content area constrained to ~1140–1200px, split into a wider main content column on the left for the events results list and a narrower right column functioning as a sidebar for filters and search. Within the main column, the sort control sits at the top, directly above the events results list. Within the sidebar, the filter bar (including keyword search / general filters) appears at the top, with location and date/time filters stacked beneath it.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a top-to-bottom, full-width structure that mirrors the mockup: (1) a dark full-width header bar containing the primary navigation; (2) a full-width hero section for the Events page with a photograph and overlaid headline; (3) below the hero, a centered content area constrained to ~1140–1200px, split into a wider main content column on the left for the events results list and a narrower right column functioning as a sidebar for filters and search. Within the main column, the sort control sits at the top, directly above the events results list. Within the sidebar, the filter bar (including keyword search / general filters) appears at the top, with location and date/time filters stacked beneath it.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the header occupies roughly 20–25% of the initial viewport height, the hero takes about 55–65%, and the top of the main content + sidebar layout begins in the remaining 10–15%. Horizontally, the primary navigation inside the header reserves ~30% for the logo/brand on the left and ~70% for navigation links on the right. Below, the centered content grid allocates about 65–70% of the width (≈8 out of 12 columns) to the main events results and 30–35% (≈4 out of 12 columns) to the right-hand filters sidebar. All sections respect an 8px-based spacing system with 48–64px between major sections (header–hero–content) and 16–24px between internal groups (filters, cards, list items).
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a moderate content density. The header and hero are visually bold but contain relatively few elements, with generous padding and whitespace to feel clean and editorial. The filters in the sidebar are denser, stacked vertically but separated by 16–24px to avoid clutter. The events results list uses card-based items with clear padding, single-column layout, and 16–24px gaps between cards. Status elements (loading and empty state) appear in the same main content region but use more open space and centered alignment to keep them immediately legible and visually distinct.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F- and Z-pattern. The eye starts at the top-left logo in the dark header, then moves horizontally to the right across the navigation links. From there, it drops diagonally to the Events hero headline over the photograph, placed left-of-center around the lower third of the hero. The user’s gaze then moves down into the main content, first catching the sort control at the upper-right of the main column and the top of the events list, and then flows vertically down the list of event cards. Concurrently, the right-hand sidebar filter bar and its stacked filters create a secondary focal trail along the right edge, drawing the eye downwards and ending near the bottom-right, where a floating WhatsApp contact button (not part of these components, but present in the design) overlaps the lower-right corner of the viewport.

layout.overall_description: The Events page presents as a modern, high-contrast layout with a dark, full-width header at the very top containing the primary navigation, logo on the left and spaced navigation links on the right. Immediately beneath, a full-width hero image for the Events page fills most of the visible screen, with a large white “Events” headline and a subtle accent line positioned slightly left-of-center and in the lower third of the photograph, echoing the editorial feel of the mockup. Below this hero, the content narrows into a centered 12-column grid, where the left ~70% forms the main events area and the right ~30% forms a sidebar. At the top of the main column sits a discreet sort control aligned toward the right, followed by a vertical stack of spacious event cards. In the sidebar, a card-like filter bar with keyword search anchors the top, with location and date/time filters stacked beneath, each in its own tidy group. The spacing between these blocks uses consistent 8px-based increments, giving every element room to breathe while still feeling cohesive. When results are loading, a centered loading indicator overlays the main list; when no events match, an empty state message occupies the main column with generous whitespace. The overall impression mirrors the mockup: a bold, slightly corporate hero experience that transitions into a structured, functional browsing and filtering area, with attention naturally drawn from top-left navigation to hero, then down into events content and supporting filters on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary navigation is embedded in a full-width, black (or very dark) header bar pinned to the very top of the page. Within this bar, content is horizontally centered inside a max-width container of approximately 1140–1200px. The left 30% of this container is reserved for the church/initiative logo and name, aligned to the far left with 16–24px padding from the container edge. The right 70% contains the navigation links, arranged in a single horizontal row, right-aligned with even spacing between each item. Vertical padding in the header is generous (16–24px top and bottom), giving the navigation items a comfortable click area and matching the moderately spacious look of the mockup. On scroll, the header remains at the top of the viewport while the hero and page content scroll beneath it, preserving quick access to navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-width, horizontally centered content within a fixed-height band at the top of the viewport; remains visible at the top as the user scrolls (sticky or fixed header), with content scrolling underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events URL state manager is a non-visual, structural component that sits at the page level, logically wrapping the Events main content region (filters and results). It does not occupy any visible space or alter the grid directly; instead, it lives ‘behind’ the scenes, mediating the state shared between the filter controls in the right sidebar, the sort control above the results, and the events results list itself. Spatially, it can be thought of as attached to the entire main content section that begins just below the hero, stretching the full width of the centered content container but with zero height and no visual affordances.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual container with no physical footprint; logically spans the main content area below the hero to coordinate state without affecting layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events filter bar is positioned at the very top of the right-hand sidebar column, starting just below the hero section’s bottom edge. Within the 12-column grid, it occupies the rightmost 4 columns (approximately 30–35% of the content width). It appears as a card-like block with a subtle background (e.g., light gray or soft white against the page background), 16–24px of internal padding, and slightly rounded corners. A concise title like “Filter Events” (if present) is aligned to the top-left inside this block, with a primary keyword search or combined filter input spanning nearly the full width beneath it. Vertical spacing between the heading, search field, and any additional inline controls follows the 8–16px increments. The filter bar’s top aligns horizontally with the top of the main column’s sort control so that both columns begin at roughly the same vertical position below the hero, reinforcing the tidy, balanced split between main content and sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar column; scrolls with the page as part of the sidebar content and maintains its relative position at the top of the sidebar on load.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events location filter sits directly beneath the events filter bar in the sidebar, separated by a vertical gap of about 16–24px to create a clear grouping hierarchy. It uses the same width as the sidebar (rightmost 4 columns) and presents as another compact card-like block, visually related to the filter bar through consistent background, padding, and border or shadow style. Inside, a label such as “Location” is aligned top-left, with its interactive controls (dropdown, tags, checkboxes, etc.) stacked vertically beneath. Each control line has 8–12px vertical spacing to prevent a cramped look. The left edges of the label and controls align with the left padding of the card, maintaining a crisp, columnar alignment that matches the overall grid. The bottom of this card leaves a 16–24px margin before the next filter section to keep the sidebar easy to scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the right sidebar; vertically stacked under the filter bar and above the date/time filter, scrolling naturally with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events date/time filter is the next block in the sidebar stack, placed directly below the location filter with a 16–24px vertical gap. It occupies the same rightmost 4 columns, matching the card-like treatment of the other filters (similar padding, background, and border/shadow). A label like “Date & Time” appears at the top-left of the block, aligned with the labels above, with its controls (date range picker, quick presets, or toggles) arranged either in a simple two-column layout inside the card or stacked vertically depending on complexity, always respecting the 8px spacing base unit. The internal layout is kept clean and linear so the sidebar reads as a vertical series of well-separated filter modules. The bottom of this card has enough breathing room (at least 24px) before any subsequent sidebar content or the footer area begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the right sidebar stack; scrolls with the sidebar and maintains alignment with the other filter blocks.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events sort control is positioned at the top of the main content column, just below the hero and horizontally aligned with the top of the sidebar’s filter bar. Within the centered 12-column grid, it sits in the left ~8 columns but is visually aligned to the right edge of that main column, often occupying about 25–35% of the main column’s width (e.g., a right-aligned dropdown or segmented control). There is 8–16px padding or margin above and below it to maintain separation from the hero above and the first row of results below. On larger screens, this creates a clear F-pattern: after scanning the hero, the eye is pulled to this subtle control on the upper-right of the main content area before dropping down into the results. On smaller screens where columns stack, the sort control moves to the top of the stacked content, just above filters or results depending on the mobile strategy, but always near the top of the content area and with consistent horizontal padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline control at the top of the main column; scrolls away with the content. On smaller viewports, it reflows above the results list in a single-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events results list occupies the main content column to the left of the sidebar, spanning roughly 8 of the 12 columns (about 65–70% of the centered page width). It begins directly below the events sort control, with a 16–24px vertical gap separating the two. The list displays event cards in a single vertical stack, alignment flush with the left edge of the main column and leaving a comfortable right margin to the sidebar. Each result card takes the full width of this column, and the cards are separated from one another by 16–24px of vertical spacing, keeping the list readable and rhythmically spaced. The list area has extra bottom padding (32–48px) before the page footer to prevent a cramped end. When the page first loads or filters change, this list region is the area over which loading and empty states will appear, ensuring that the user understands that feedback refers to this results area specifically.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list occupying the left main column; scrolls within the page. Loading and empty-state overlays appear centered inside this list region, without shifting the sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each events result card is a full-width card within the main column of the results list, constrained by that column’s width (approximately 8 grid columns). Cards have generous internal padding (16–24px) and a subtle background or border to separate them from the page background. Content within a card flows from top to bottom: event name as a bold title aligned to the top-left, followed by date/time and location metadata arranged in a compact horizontal row or tight vertical stack using 4–8px spacing. Below this, a short description or call-to-action is placed with 8–12px spacing from the metadata. An optional primary action button (e.g., “View Details”) is aligned bottom-right within the card, while secondary information remains left-aligned, creating a slight diagonal emphasis. The card edges align with the main column’s left and right padding to maintain a consistent vertical spine with other cards. Vertical space between cards in the list is 16–24px, so the stack feels orderly and easy to scan rather than dense or noisy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level items stacked vertically inside the results list; they expand vertically based on content but always occupy the full width of the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events empty state appears within the same footprint as the events results list when there are no events to show. Instead of multiple cards, a single centered panel occupies the main column, beginning roughly at the same vertical position where the first card would normally appear. It aligns with the left and right edges of the main column (≈8 columns wide) but uses more vertical whitespace: at least 32–48px of padding inside the panel and generous top margin relative to the sort control. A simple icon or illustration can be placed at the center, with the main message directly beneath it and any secondary guidance (e.g., “Try changing your filters”) below that, each separated by 8–12px. The sidebar filters remain visible to the right, emphasizing that the user can adjust them to find events. The empty state does not alter the sidebar layout; it only replaces the list content area to the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual replacement of the results list content in the main column; static while visible and scrolls normally with the page. Does not overlay or obscure the filters sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events loading indicator is positioned within the main content column, centered over the region where the events results list cards normally appear. When active, it overlays this area with a subtle dimming layer (e.g., semi-transparent background) and a spinner or progress indicator centered both horizontally and vertically within the visible portion of the main column. The overlay respects the left and right boundaries of the main column (≈8 columns) and does not extend over the sidebar, allowing the filters on the right to remain visually stable. There is additional padding above and below the indicator to keep it visually detached from the sort control and page footer. Once loading completes, this indicator disappears completely, returning full visibility to the list or empty state beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the events results list area in the main column, using absolute positioning relative to that container; it scrolls off-screen if the user has scrolled away from the list region while loading.

name: Event Detail
title: Event Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a single event, allows visitors to register or RSVP, and lets them add eligible events to their personal outreach plan.

core_features: Load and display full event details based on the eventId URL parameter using getEventDetail.
Show basic event information: title, category, date and time, campus/location, format, and status.
Show practical details: cost, activity level, requirements, and full address (including map-ready coordinates when provided).
Provide a registration or RSVP form that posts to registerForEvent, collecting name, email, optional phone, and number of attendees.
Determine whether to label the form as required registration or optional RSVP based on the event’s isRegistrationRequired flag from getEventDetail.
Display an on-page confirmation message after a successful registration/RSVP response from registerForEvent.
Expose an 'Add to my outreach plan' action for events where canAddToOutreachPlan is true, calling addEventToOutreachPlan.
Allow an optional personal note when adding the event to the outreach plan and confirm success inline on the page.
Provide navigation links back to the Events listing and forward to the Outreach Plan page after adding an event.
Handle error and edge states for missing/invalid eventId, cancelled/completed events, and failed API calls while keeping the page functional.
user_workflows: View event details: (1) User navigates to event_detail.html with an eventId URL parameter. (2) The page reads eventId from the URL query string. (3) The page calls getEventDetail(eventId). (4) When getEventDetail returns, the page shows event title, category label, date/time, cost, format, activity level, requirements, and location information. (5) If status indicates the event is cancelled or completed, a status message is shown and registration actions are disabled.

Register or RSVP for an event: (1) User scrolls to the registration section. (2) The page uses the isRegistrationRequired value from getEventDetail to set the registration type to either 'registration' (required) or 'rsvp' (optional). (3) User enters first name, last name, email, optional phone, and number of attendees. (4) User submits the form. (5) The page calls registerForEvent with eventId (from URL), registrationType (derived from event details), name fields, email, phone, and numAttendees. (6) If registerForEvent returns success=true, the page displays a success message and key registration details while clearing or locking the form. (7) If success=false, the page shows the message returned by the API and keeps the form fields so the user can correct and resubmit.

Add an event to the outreach plan: (1) User views an event where canAddToOutreachPlan from getEventDetail is true. (2) The page displays an 'Add to my outreach plan' button and an optional note input. (3) User optionally enters a brief note about why or how they plan to serve. (4) User clicks the add-to-plan button. (5) The page calls addEventToOutreachPlan with eventId (from URL) and the note text (or an empty string if none provided). (6) If addEventToOutreachPlan returns success=true, the page shows a confirmation message including the plan title or other summary information returned. (7) The confirmation area also shows a link to outreach_plan.html that the user can click to view and manage their overall plan. (8) If success=false, the page shows the error message and keeps the note input so the user can try again.

Navigate between events and other sections: (1) From the Events listing or Home page, the user clicks an event and lands on event_detail.html with eventId in the URL. (2) From the Event Detail page, clicking 'Back to events' navigates the user to events.html without additional parameters. (3) After adding an event to the outreach plan, clicking 'View my outreach plan' navigates to outreach_plan.html so the user can see their full list of planned events.
interactions: On page load, read the eventId query parameter from the URL and trigger getEventDetail(eventId); show a loading state until the call resolves or fails.

Primary navigation interaction: use getPrimaryNavigation to load navigation items; clicking each item navigates the browser to the corresponding site section URL (resolved outside this page’s interfaces).
Clicking the 'Back to events' text or button triggers a navigation to events.html without calling any API on this page.

Registration/R.S.V.P form interactions: users type into text inputs for first name, last name, email, phone (optional), and number of attendees; client-side validation runs on field blur and on form submit (e.g., required fields, positive attendee count).

Submitting the registration/RSVP form triggers an onSubmit handler that calls registerForEvent with the current form values and the eventId from the URL; while the call is in progress the submit button is disabled to prevent duplicate submissions.

After registerForEvent returns, the page shows either a success banner with the message and registration details or an inline error message; for a success, the form can be reset or disabled based on configuration, but the confirmation remains visible on the page.
If the event status from getEventDetail is cancelled or completed, the registration form section is visually disabled or hidden, and a status message explains that registration is not available.

Outreach plan action interactions: when canAddToOutreachPlan is true, users can enter an optional note and click the 'Add to my outreach plan' button; this triggers addEventToOutreachPlan with eventId and note.
While addEventToOutreachPlan is in progress, the add button is disabled or replaced with a loading indicator to avoid duplicate adds.
After addEventToOutreachPlan completes successfully, a confirmation message appears along with a 'View my outreach plan' link; clicking that link navigates to outreach_plan.html.

Error handling interactions: if getEventDetail fails or returns data indicating the event cannot be displayed, the page shows an error message and hides dependent components such as the registration form and outreach plan action.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by the eventId URL query parameter (for example, event_detail.html?eventId={eventId}). On initial load, the page reads eventId from the URL. If eventId is present, the page calls getEventDetail(eventId) to populate all event-specific components; if eventId is missing or invalid, the page shows an error state and does not attempt registration or outreach plan actions. The getEventDetail response determines several derived UI states: (1) registrationType is set to 'registration' when isRegistrationRequired is true, otherwise 'rsvp'; this value is passed to registerForEvent; (2) the registration form and outreach plan button are only enabled when status is 'scheduled'; if status is 'cancelled' or 'completed', these actions are disabled and a status message is displayed; (3) the outreach plan action is shown only when canAddToOutreachPlan is true; when false, the button and note input are hidden or replaced with an informational message. The success or failure responses from registerForEvent and addEventToOutreachPlan update in-memory page state for confirmation messages and do not modify the URL. Navigation to events.html and outreach_plan.html is done via standard links without additional query parameters from this page. No localStorage or other persistent client-side storage is used; all state needed for this page is derived from the URL and the results of the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Loads and renders the site’s main navigation labels in the header using getPrimaryNavigation, allowing users to move to other site sections.
event_handlers: onInit: call getPrimaryNavigation() and store the returned navigation items in component state.
onNavItemClick: navigate the browser to the appropriate page for the clicked navigation item (URL resolution handled outside this API).

components.functionality: Reads the eventId from the URL query string and loads event details via getEventDetail; exposes loading, success, and error states to downstream components.
event_handlers: onInit: parse eventId from window.location.search; if present, call getEventDetail(eventId) and store the result; if missing, set an error state.
onReloadEvent: if triggered (e.g., manual retry), re-call getEventDetail(eventId) and update bound data and error states.

components.functionality: Displays top-level event information such as title, category label, date, time, campus name, and overall status using data loaded by event-detail-loader.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: when event data from event-detail-loader is updated, re-render the title, category, date/time, campusName, and status label.
onBackToEventsClick: navigate to events.html when the user clicks the 'Back to events' link.

components.functionality: Shows the full event description, location details, cost information, format, activity level, and any requirements text.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: update description, costDisplay, formatLabel, activityLevelLabel, requirements, address fields, and any map-related attributes when event details change.
onMapLinkClick: if latitude and longitude are present, construct and open an external map link based on those coordinates (implementation outside the scope of this API).

components.functionality: Shows contextual status messages based on the event status and other flags (scheduled, cancelled, completed) and controls the availability of interactive components like registration and outreach actions.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: inspect status and other fields from getEventDetail; set internal flags such as isRegistrationAllowed and isOutreachActionAllowed.
onStatusFlagChange: notify dependent components (registration form, outreach plan action) when actions should be enabled or disabled based on current event status.

components.functionality: Collects attendee information for the event and submits a registration or RSVP via registerForEvent, choosing the correct registration type based on event settings.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: determine registrationType by reading isRegistrationRequired from getEventDetail; set registrationType to 'registration' when true, otherwise 'rsvp'; also disable the form if status is not 'scheduled'.
onInputChange: update form state as the user types firstName, lastName, email, phone, and numAttendees.
onNumAttendeesChange: enforce non-negative, non-zero numeric input before submission.

onSubmit: validate required fields; if valid, call registerForEvent with eventId (from URL), registrationType (derived from event data), firstName, lastName, fullName (composed from first and last name), email, phone, and numAttendees; while awaiting the response, disable the submit button.

onSubmitSuccess: when registerForEvent returns success=true, store the returned registration object, display a success message via the confirmation component, and optionally reset or lock the form fields.
onSubmitError: when registerForEvent returns success=false or an error occurs, display the returned message or a generic error, keep current form values, and re-enable the submit button.

components.functionality: Shows confirmation or error feedback related to event registration or RSVP submissions.
event_handlers: onRegistrationResponse: listen for the result of registerForEvent from event-registration-form; when success, display the API message along with key registration fields such as email and numAttendees.
onDismissMessageClick: allow the user to hide the confirmation or error message without affecting the underlying registration data.

components.functionality: Allows the user to add the current event to their outreach plan by calling addEventToOutreachPlan, with an optional personal note.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: check canAddToOutreachPlan from getEventDetail; show or hide the add-to-plan controls based on this flag and on event status.
onNoteChange: update the local note value as the user types.

onAddToPlanClick: when the button is clicked and the event is eligible, call addEventToOutreachPlan with eventId (from URL) and the current note (or an empty string if none); disable the button while the request is pending.

onAddToPlanSuccess: when addEventToOutreachPlan returns success=true, store outreachPlan and planItem from the response and show a success confirmation via the outreach-plan-confirmation component; optionally disable the button to prevent duplicate adds.
onAddToPlanError: display the message from the failed addEventToOutreachPlan call and re-enable the button so the user can retry.

components.functionality: Displays the result of adding an event to the outreach plan and provides navigation to the Outreach Plan page.
event_handlers: onPlanItemCreated: when a successful addEventToOutreachPlan response is received, render the confirmation message using outreachPlan.title, planItem.eventTitle, and planItem.addedAt as appropriate.
onViewOutreachPlanClick: navigate the user to outreach_plan.html when they click 'View my outreach plan'.
onDismissMessageClick: allow the user to hide the outreach-plan confirmation message without affecting the underlying plan data.

components.functionality: Provides contextual navigation from the event detail page to related areas, including the Events list and (when appropriate) the user’s outreach plan.
event_handlers: onBackToEventsClick: navigate to events.html when the back link is activated.

onConditionalOutreachPlanLinkRender: show a 'View my outreach plan' link when an event has just been added to the outreach plan (based on the success response from addEventToOutreachPlan); clicking it navigates to outreach_plan.html.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid typical of WordPress themes, with content max-width likely around 1140–1200px inside full-width sections.
layout.layout_pattern: Adopt the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width dark header containing the primary navigation at the very top, followed immediately by a full-width hero-style event summary header. Below the hero, the page shifts into a two-column main content area centered within a max-width container (~1140–1200px): a wider left column for the event details, status, and registration, and a narrower right column acting as a functional sidebar for outreach-plan actions. Feedback panels appear directly beneath their related forms. Global event navigation links sit at the bottom, spanning the full content width.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Adopt the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width dark header containing the primary navigation at the very top, followed immediately by a full-width hero-style event summary header. Below the hero, the page shifts into a two-column main content area centered within a max-width container (~1140–1200px): a wider left column for the event details, status, and registration, and a narrower right column acting as a functional sidebar for outreach-plan actions. Feedback panels appear directly beneath their related forms. Global event navigation links sit at the bottom, spanning the full content width.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Preserve the observed proportions: the dark header occupies roughly 20–25% of the initial viewport height, the hero event summary ~55–65%, and the beginning of the two-column content occupies the remaining ~10–15% above the fold. Horizontally, the main content column takes about 65–70% of the content width, while the sidebar occupies about 30–35%. Vertical spacing between major sections (header → hero → main content → footer links) uses 48–64px gaps; internal spacing within components and between related blocks (form fields, status messages, feedback panels) uses 16–24px based on the 8px spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderate density: the hero remains visually bold but minimal (few text elements over an image), the header navigation is spacious, and the main content is organized into clearly separated blocks (details, form, actions) with strong grouping and ample padding. Interactive elements like forms and action cards are compact enough to feel functional, but each is set within a generous card-style container so nothing feels cramped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Follow the mockup’s F/Z-pattern. The eye begins at the top-left logo in the dark header, moves right across the primary navigation, then drops diagonally down into the bright event title in the hero. From there, the gaze flows down the left column through event details, status, and registration form. In parallel, the right-hand sidebar card with the outreach-plan action and call-to-action button acts as a secondary focal point. Bright confirmation panels and the lower event navigation links gently pull the eye toward the bottom of the page as the journey concludes.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a full-width, nearly black header bar stretching across the top, with the initiative’s wordmark anchored to the left and evenly spaced navigation links on the right, all sitting within a centered max-width content frame. Directly below, a full-bleed hero banner features a photographic background, with the event name and key meta information overlaid slightly left of center and around the lower third of the image, visually echoing an editorial magazine cover. As visitors scroll, the layout resolves into a centered two-column grid: a broader left column holds the narrative of the event and the registration flow, while a slimmer right sidebar houses a prominent card to add the event to a personal outreach plan. Status messages and confirmation panels appear inline, closely following the forms they relate to. At the very bottom, a low-density strip of event navigation links spans the full content width, tying the page back into the broader event list. Overall the layout feels modern, focused, and structured, with the hero and left column carrying the story and the right sidebar providing clear, action-oriented support.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary navigation sits as a fixed-height, full-width bar at the very top of the page, with a dark, nearly black background. Inside a centered max-width container (~1140–1200px), the logo or site name is aligned flush to the left, taking roughly the left 25–30% of the header’s width, while the navigation links are right-aligned within the remaining 70–75%. Vertically, the bar is around 72–88px tall, giving enough height for comfortable 16–20px top and bottom padding around the nav items. The header’s bottom edge meets the top edge of the hero section without any visible gap, maintaining a clean, continuous top frame.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document flow (not sticky). Always spans 100% of the viewport width, with content constrained to the central max-width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-detail-loader appears as a semi-transparent overlay directly above the event-summary-header and the main two-column content, but below the primary navigation. It covers the full width of the content area and the full height from the bottom of the header down to below the fold as needed. At its center (both vertically and horizontally), a minimal loader indicator (spinner or progress icon with optional short text) is displayed, with generous padding of 24–32px around it. When visible, it subtly dims the hero image and main content while preserving their underlying structure, signaling that the event data is loading without visually disrupting the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as a full-width, full-height overlay relative to the main content region, with a high z-index over the hero and main sections but under the header. It appears and disappears dynamically based on loading state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-summary-header functions as the hero banner and sits directly beneath the primary navigation, spanning the full browser width edge-to-edge. Its height on desktop is roughly 55–65% of the viewport height, creating a strong visual presence. A full-bleed photograph related to the outreach or event fills the background. Centered within this area is a max-width content column aligned with the header’s content; within that, the event meta (date, time, location) appears as compact text toward the upper-left of the content column, while the main event title is overlaid slightly left of center, positioned in the lower third of the hero. The title is large and high-contrast (white or near-white) with an accent underline or small accent bar just beneath it, echoing the mockup’s editorial style. There is 24–32px of padding above and below these hero texts relative to the internal content bounds to keep the hero feeling open and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width section. The background image scales responsively to cover the hero area, while the text content stays within the centered max-width container aligned to the overall grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-details-section is the first major block of the main content region and sits immediately below the hero, separated by a vertical gap of about 48–64px to create a clear transition. The main content area is centered within the page and constrained to the same max-width as the header and hero content. Within this area, a two-column grid is established: the left column, which contains the event-details-section, occupies about 65–70% of the width, while the right column (sidebar) occupies about 30–35%. The event-details-section sits at the very top of the left column, starting flush with the column’s left edge, and spans its full width. It includes the detailed event description, schedule, location details, and any additional context laid out in a linear, top-to-bottom flow with 16–24px vertical spacing between subheadings, paragraphs, and list items. The section maintains a clean, text-forward presentation without a heavy card border, relying instead on consistent typography and spacing to define its structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column of the two-column grid. It sets the vertical starting point for subsequent left-column components like status messages and the registration form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-status-messages component appears in the left column directly beneath the event-details-section and just above the event-registration-form, using a vertical gap of about 24–32px from the bottom of the details content. It presents as one or more horizontal alert-style bands or a compact card spanning the full width of the left column. Each message is contained within a lightly colored background panel (e.g., soft green for available, amber for limited, muted red for closed/cancelled) with 12–16px of padding on all sides and 8–12px rounded corners. The panel is visually separated from the surrounding text with 16px of margin above and below, making it clearly scannable as a status indicator. The overall height is kept modest so it reads as a quick state cue just before the user reaches the registration form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline component in the left column. Its presence and color vary based on the event’s state, but its position relative to the details section and registration form remains fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-registration-form is positioned in the left column directly beneath the event-status-messages, with about 24–32px of vertical spacing between them. It is presented as a distinct form card that spans the full width of the left column, with a subtle border or shadow and 24–32px internal padding to reflect its importance while keeping a clean, minimal feel. A concise form title or label appears at the top of the card, followed by a vertical stack of form fields (name, contact information, attendee count, etc.) aligned left and arranged with 16–24px vertical spacing between field groups. Primary action buttons (e.g., "Register" or "RSVP") sit at the bottom right of the form card, with sufficient top margin (16–24px) to separate them from the last field. The form occupies roughly the middle third of the page’s vertical scroll when in view, forming a natural focal area after reading the event details and status.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column. The form height expands as needed based on fields, but always maintains consistent padding and alignment within the column grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-registration-confirmation panel appears in close proximity to the event-registration-form. When a registration is successfully submitted, this feedback panel appears directly below the form card, separated from the form by 16–24px of vertical space, and spans the full width of the left column. It resembles a clean, compact notification card with a success accent (e.g., a thin colored bar on the left or a soft green background strip across the top), 16–24px internal padding, and a brief confirmation message plus any next-step guidance. Its height is relatively shallow compared to the form, so it reads as a clear but unobtrusive confirmation at the end of the interaction sequence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element anchored below the registration form in the left column, appearing conditionally after submission. It does not overlay other content; instead, it pushes subsequent content downward when rendered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The outreach-plan-action component lives in the right-hand sidebar column, aligned to start horizontally where the event-details-section begins in the left column. It is positioned at the top of the sidebar with a vertical offset from the bottom of the hero matching the left column’s top margin (about 48–64px), ensuring both columns feel aligned. This component appears as a prominent action card occupying the full width of the sidebar (roughly 30–35% of the content width). The card includes a concise title (e.g., "Add to Your Outreach Plan"), a short explanatory line, and one primary button plus any supporting controls, all arranged vertically with 12–16px spacing. The card uses slightly stronger visual treatment than standard informational boxes—perhaps a more noticeable border, a light tinted background, or a vertical accent strip—so it stands out as an actionable secondary focal point alongside the registration form. There is 24–32px padding inside the card and 24px of margin below it to accommodate additional sidebar content if needed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves like a sticky sidebar card on larger screens: once the user scrolls beyond the hero, the outreach-plan-action card remains visible pinned near the top of the viewport within the right column, until the main content ends. On smaller viewports, it falls back to normal document flow and may stack beneath the left column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The outreach-plan-confirmation appears in the same sidebar column directly associated with the outreach-plan-action card. When the user successfully adds the event to their outreach plan, this feedback panel is displayed beneath the outreach-plan-action card, separated by about 16–24px. It spans the full width of the sidebar and is visually treated as a compact confirmation card, with a soft success color accent, 12–16px of internal padding, and a concise confirmation message. Its size and styling are slightly more understated than the outreach-plan-action card so that the action card remains the primary element while the confirmation reads as a supportive, reassuring message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element that appears under the outreach-plan-action card in the sidebar. It scrolls together with the sidebar content and respects any sticky behavior of the parent card (remaining visually attached beneath it).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-navigation-links component appears at the very bottom of the main content area, after both the left and right column content have concluded. It spans the full width of the centered max-width container and is separated from the content above by a generous vertical gap of 48–64px, functioning like a low-density footer for the page’s event-specific content. Within this strip, navigation links such as "Back to Events," "Previous Event," and "Next Event" are laid out horizontally. The primary contextual link (e.g., "Back to Events") is aligned to the left, while optional previous/next links may be right-aligned, creating a subtle horizontal tension that mirrors the header’s left-right structure. Padding of 16–24px vertically gives the links breathing room, and the overall styling remains minimal—simple text links or lightly styled buttons—so they don’t compete with the hero or main CTAs but still provide clear onward paths.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the main content, sitting above any global site footer. It does not stick or float; it is the natural end of the event detail layout.

name: Give
title: Give
description: Page for making one-time or recurring donations, allocating gifts across funds, entering donor and payment details, and receiving confirmation.

core_features: Load primary site navigation items for consistent header navigation using getPrimaryNavigation.
Initialize giving options (frequencies, available funds, allocation modes, payment methods) using getGivingFormOptions.
Support selection of gift frequency (e.g., one-time or recurring) based on options returned by getGivingFormOptions.
Enable multi-fund giving by allowing donors to add, edit, and remove allocation rows tied to funds from getGivingFormOptions.funds.
Respect optional incoming URL parameters (fundId, suggestedAmount) to preselect a fund and/or a suggested gift amount in the first allocation row.
Collect donor contact information (name, email, mailing address) required for processing and receipts.
Collect payment information appropriate to the selected payment method (e.g., card number, expiration, CVV, billing postal code for card payments).
Validate gift details (required fields, positive amounts, allocation totals) before submission.
Submit the donation via submitDonation, including allocations and donor/payment details, and handle the success or failure response.
Display a donation confirmation area showing confirmation number, total amount, frequency, and allocation summary from submitDonation, with an action to return to the home page.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Standard one-time gift without preselected fund
1. User navigates to give.html via header or footer navigation (no URL parameters used).
2. Page calls getPrimaryNavigation to render the site navigation.
3. Page calls getGivingFormOptions to populate frequency options, available funds, allocation modes, and payment methods.
