Continue down into the left column’s services and notes (primary reading area), with the right sidebar forming a strong secondary focal zone containing contact info, hours, and action buttons clustered toward the upper and mid-right. Status banners briefly intercept attention just beneath the nav, and action-oriented elements on the right (e.g., call or directions buttons) echo the mockup’s bottom-right interaction emphasis.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a modern, full-width layout with a strong, slightly corporate nonprofit tone. A dark, high-contrast header stretches edge-to-edge across the top, carrying the church initiative’s logo on the left and well-spaced navigation links on the right. Immediately below, a photo-driven hero for the selected food pantry fills most of the above-the-fold area, with the pantry name and a few key qualifiers overlaid just left of center in the lower third of the image, evoking the editorial strength of the original mockup. Beneath this hero, content snaps into a two-column grid within a centered, max-width container: the left column is the main storytelling area, listing services offered and important notes in stacked, clearly separated sections, while the right column forms a functional sidebar with the address, map cues, hours of operation, and a compact action panel. Status messages slide in just below the global header to communicate availability or alerts without breaking the layout rhythm, and a subtle loader can overlay the hero and top of the content area as data is fetched. The spacing is airy yet intentional, with consistent 8px-based increments and 48–64px gaps between major sections, resulting in a page that feels organized, easy to scan, and faithful to the mockup’s strong top bar + hero + sidebar visual pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary navigation header is a full-width, horizontally oriented dark bar fixed to the top of the page viewport. It spans 100% of the browser width and sits at the very top, with no margin above and 16–24px of vertical padding inside. Within a centered max-width container (~1140–1200px), the logo/brand block occupies the left ~30% of the header area, aligned to the far left with enough padding (24px) from the viewport edge to feel anchored. The navigation links are arranged in a horizontal row aligned to the right, taking up the remaining ~70% width, each with generous horizontal spacing (16–24px between items) to mirror the mockup’s clean, modern feel. The header casts a subtle shadow or uses contrast to separate it from the hero below. On scroll, it remains fixed so that navigation is always visible while the hero and content scroll underneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the top of the viewport, full-width; other content scrolls beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status messages banner is positioned directly below the primary navigation header and above the hero-style food resource header. It sits within the same central content width as the main page (max ~1140–1200px) but feels like a thin strip cutting across the top of the content area. Vertically, it uses modest height—roughly 48–64px total—driven by text content, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. The banner is aligned to the center of the page with equal left and right margins, and stretches nearly the entire container width (90–100%), leaving a narrow margin to the edges for a neat, card-like effect if needed. When present, it is the first element a user sees after the header; when absent, the hero section immediately follows the header with no visible gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element that appears and disappears based on status; it pushes subsequent content down when visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data fetcher’s visual representation appears as a centered overlay within the hero and upper content area when the page is in a loading state. It anchors itself to the main content region starting just below the primary navigation header (and status banner, if visible). The loader occupies roughly the central 40–50% of the viewport width, with its core indicator (spinner or skeleton placeholder) centered horizontally and vertically within the hero/header zone. A subtle translucent backdrop extends over the hero and the top portion of the main content area beneath, slightly dimming underlying elements to communicate inactivity while still showing the overall layout. The overlay respects the fixed header, starting just below it so as not to cover navigation. On smaller screens, the loader remains centered and may cover most of the visible content area below the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Transient overlay positioned over the hero and upper content area; appears during loading and blocks interaction beneath while visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The food resource header acts as the hero section for this detail page. It sits directly below the status messages (or directly under the navigation header if no status is shown) and spans 100% of the viewport width, edge-to-edge. The height is visually generous, occupying roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport height on desktop, but can shrink proportionally on smaller screens to maintain readability. A full-bleed background image representing the food pantry or community setting fills this section. Within the image, a content container aligned to the global max width (~1140–1200px) is centered horizontally. The main pantry title is positioned left-of-center, typically in the lower third of the hero: imagine the text starting about 20–25% in from the left edge of the container and sitting about 60–65% down from the top of the hero. Below the title, a bold accent line (e.g., an underline or colored rule) runs horizontally at the title’s baseline, echoing the mockup’s red/magenta underline. Supporting details (such as neighborhood, category, or a brief descriptor) can sit immediately below the title with a 8–16px gap, aligned to the same left edge. Generous padding (24–48px) separates the text block from the edges of the image area. On scroll, the entire hero scrolls away as a single block, revealing the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width hero section anchored under the header area; no fixed behavior, scrolls with page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services list is placed in the left main column immediately below the hero section, forming the top of the primary content area. The overall content container is centered on the page with a max width of ~1140–1200px and uses a 12-column grid. The left column, where this component sits, occupies roughly 8 of 12 columns (~65–70% of the width). Horizontally, the services list aligns to the left edge of this column with 16–24px internal padding and a slight card or section boundary to distinguish it from the background. Vertically, it starts about 48–64px below the bottom of the hero, leaving generous white space that visually separates the hero from the detailed content. Within the block, the section title (e.g., “Services offered”) appears at the top with a comfortable 8–16px gap above the list items. Individual services are listed vertically with moderate spacing (8–12px between rows) to maintain a balanced density. The component’s bottom edge sits 24–32px above the next section (notes), preserving a clear hierarchy in the reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block anchored in the left main column; participates in normal document flow and stacks above the notes panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notes panel is located directly beneath the services list in the left main column, continuing the vertical reading flow. It aligns flush with the services list in the grid, occupying the same ~65–70% width of the page (8 of 12 columns) within the centered container. A vertical gap of approximately 24–32px separates it from the services component above, providing a clear visual break while keeping the content associated. The panel itself has a slightly more text-dense feel—longer paragraphs or bullet points—so it uses generous internal padding (16–24px all around) and a clear section heading (e.g., “Important notes” or “What to know before you visit”) at the top. Text lines are set with comfortable line spacing and align left. The bottom of this notes panel leaves a 48–64px margin to the end of the main content area or any subsequent sections, preserving the airy, editorial tone of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the left main column; scrolls with the page and expands vertically based on content length.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact info block is positioned at the top of the right sidebar column, aligning vertically with the top of the services list on the left. Within the 12-column grid, the sidebar spans roughly 4 of 12 columns (~30–35% width) and sits flush to the right edge of the main content container. A vertical offset of approximately 48–64px from the bottom of the hero mirrors the spacing used on the left to ensure visual alignment between columns. The contact block appears as a compact, card-like section with a clear heading (e.g., “Location & Contact”), address, phone, email, and optional map link arranged in a vertical stack. Internal padding is 16–24px. The block is deliberately concise to keep the top-right area easy to scan; it likely occupies only 20–30% of the sidebar’s visible height above the fold, leaving room below for hours and actions. A subtle border or shadow separates it from the background while maintaining alignment with other sidebar components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element occupying the top of the right sidebar column; scrolls normally with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hours list is placed directly beneath the contact info block within the right sidebar, forming the middle slice of the sidebar’s vertical stack. A vertical gap of around 16–24px separates it from the contact block to keep the sidebar visually segmented but cohesive. It maintains the same width as the contact block (~30–35% of the page width within the right column) and aligns perfectly with its left and right edges. Inside, the hours are laid out in a clean, vertically stacked format—day on the left, hours on the right—mirroring the moderate density of the overall design. There is 12–16px of vertical padding within the panel and 8–12px between each day’s entry, allowing for quick scanning. The top of this panel remains above the midline of the viewport on typical desktop screens, ensuring key information like hours is discoverable without scrolling too far.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the right sidebar; part of the sidebar’s normal vertical stacking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The action panel sits at the lower portion of the right sidebar, directly beneath the hours list, and serves as the primary interactive focal point in this column. It is separated from the hours block by a 16–24px vertical gap, maintaining the sidebar’s visual cadence. The panel’s width matches the sidebar (same ~30–35% page width) and aligns with the contact and hours blocks above. Inside the panel, primary actions such as “Call now,” “Get directions,” or “Share resource” are arranged either as a short vertical stack of full-width buttons or in one to two rows, each button spanning most of the panel’s width with 8–12px gaps between them. Buttons have 10–14px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, making them feel substantial but not oversized. The action panel begins roughly in the lower half of the sidebar, aligning with the mockup’s tendency to draw the eye to the lower-right corner for interaction. On taller screens, it may appear fully above the fold; on shorter screens, it becomes visible with a minimal scroll, still located in that right-hand interaction zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the bottom of the sidebar stack; scrolls with the page. Can optionally become sticky within the sidebar on very tall screens, but baseline behavior is normal flow.

name: Prayer Request
title: Prayer Request
description: Page where users can submit a prayer request by selecting a category, choosing a privacy level, setting a focus date, entering their message, and optionally providing their contact information. The page loads navigation, form options, and suggested dates dynamically and sends the request to the backend.

core_features: Load and display the primary site navigation in the header using getPrimaryNavigation, without hardcoding navigation items.
Load prayer request form options (categories, privacy options, suggested focus date) using getPrayerRequestFormOptions and bind them to the form inputs.
Pre-select the prayer category in the form based on the optional URL parameter categoryKey when it matches a category key returned by getPrayerRequestFormOptions.
Provide a prayer request form that lets users select a category, choose a privacy option, pick or adjust a focus date using a date picker, and enter a message with optional name and email.
Validate the form before submission (required category, privacy, focus date, and message; email format if provided) using only values returned from getPrayerRequestFormOptions and user input.
Submit the completed prayer request to the backend using submitPrayerRequest and handle the success or error response.
Display a confirmation state that shows the success message and key details from the submitPrayerRequest response, and offer actions to return to the home page or submit another request.
Handle error states for both loading form options and submitting the request, showing dynamic error messages without hardcoded error content.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Open the Prayer Request page
1) User navigates to prayer_request.html (optionally with ?categoryKey=someKey in the URL). 2) On page initialization, the page calls getPrimaryNavigation to populate the header navigation and getPrayerRequestFormOptions to retrieve categories, privacy options, and a suggested focus date. 3) After form options load, the page checks the URL parameter categoryKey; if it matches one of the loaded category keys, that category is pre-selected in the form, otherwise no category is pre-selected. 4) The user sees the fully loaded form with navigation and can begin interacting with the inputs.

Workflow: Fill out and submit a prayer request
1) User selects a category from the dynamically loaded list of categories. 2) User selects a privacy option from the dynamically loaded privacy options (a default may be selected based on list order, but the user can change it). 3) The focus date field is pre-filled with the suggestedFocusDate from getPrayerRequestFormOptions; the user can adjust this using the date picker to any valid date value the backend will accept. 4) User types their prayer message. 5) User optionally enters their name and email address. 6) User submits the form. 7) The form validates required fields and email format (if provided); if validation passes, the page calls submitPrayerRequest with the selected categoryKey, privacyKey, focusDate, message, name, and email. 8) While the request is in progress, the form indicates a submitting state and prevents duplicate submissions. 9) On a successful response with success=true, the page transitions to a confirmation view that uses the response’s message and prayerRequest object.

Workflow: Handle submission errors
1) User completes the form and clicks submit. 2) submitPrayerRequest is called. 3) If submitPrayerRequest returns success=false or the request fails (e.g., network or server error), the form remains visible and an error notification component is populated with the error message from the response (if available) or a generic, non-hardcoded fallback message defined at implementation time. 4) The user can adjust their input if needed and attempt to submit again.

Workflow: Confirmation and navigation after a successful request
1) After a successful submitPrayerRequest call, the page displays a confirmation component that shows the dynamic confirmation message and selected details such as categoryLabel, privacyLabel, and focusDate from the response prayerRequest object. 2) Within the confirmation component, the user can click a 'Return to home' action that navigates to index.html (as defined in outgoing navigation) with no additional parameters. 3) Alternatively, the user can choose an action like 'Submit another request' which resets the page state to show a fresh, reinitialized prayer-request form (reusing the latest getPrayerRequestFormOptions data).

Workflow: Arrive with a pre-selected category
1) User clicks a link from index.html or about.html that includes a categoryKey query parameter (for example, prayer_request.html?categoryKey=someKey). 2) On prayer_request.html load, the page reads the categoryKey from the URL but does not use it until after getPrayerRequestFormOptions resolves. 3) Once categories are available, the page compares categoryKey to the keys in the categories array; if a match is found, it sets that category as the selected category in the form. 4) If no match is found or the parameter is absent, the form behaves as if no category was pre-selected, and the user selects a category manually before submitting.
interactions:
Interaction: Header navigation click
- Trigger: User clicks on a navigation item in the header (e.g., Serve, Events, Give, Get Help, Care & Prayer, About, Contact), where labels and order are loaded via getPrimaryNavigation.  - Behavior: The navigation component uses the item’s id to route the user to the corresponding page URL determined by the client-side routing configuration; this behavior does not rely on hardcoded nav labels or order.  - State: No change to the prayer request form state other than the user leaving the page.

Interaction: Category selection
- Trigger: User clicks or taps the category selector in the prayer request form.  - Data: The options displayed are bound to the categories array returned from getPrayerRequestFormOptions.  - Behavior: When the user selects an option, the form’s internal state for categoryKey updates to the selected option’s key and clears any validation error associated with the category field.

Interaction: Privacy selection
- Trigger: User clicks or taps the privacy selector in the form.  - Data: Options are bound to privacyOptions from getPrayerRequestFormOptions, displaying the provided label and description for each option without hardcoding them.  - Behavior: Selecting a privacy option updates the form’s privacyKey in state and clears any associated validation error.

Interaction: Focus date selection
- Trigger: User interacts with the date picker component bound to the focus date field.  - Data: The field is initialized with suggestedFocusDate from getPrayerRequestFormOptions (converted to the appropriate input format if needed) but can be changed by the user.  - Behavior: On change, the focusDate value in the form state is updated. If the new value fails client-side validation (e.g., empty or invalid format), the date picker flags the field and prevents submission until corrected.

Interaction: Message, name, and email input
- Trigger: User types into the message textarea and optional name and email fields.  - Behavior: Each keystroke updates the form state for the corresponding field. On blur (field losing focus), the form may run basic validation (e.g., required message, valid email pattern if email is not empty) and update the validation state.

Interaction: Form submit
- Trigger: User clicks the form’s submit button.  - Behavior: The form component runs client-side validation on all fields. If validation fails, it prevents submission and updates error states per field. If validation succeeds, it disables the submit button, sets a submitting state, and calls submitPrayerRequest with the current state values categoryKey, privacyKey, focusDate, message, name, and email.  - After the API call resolves, it either transitions to the confirmation state (on success) or shows an error notification (on failure), and re-enables the submit button as appropriate.

Interaction: Error notification
- Trigger: An error is detected either while loading form options via getPrayerRequestFormOptions or while submitting via submitPrayerRequest.  - Behavior: The error-notification component is populated with the error message provided from the API, if available. The user can dismiss the notification (e.g., clicking a close button), which clears the error state but does not reset form input data.

Interaction: Return to home from confirmation
- Trigger: After a successful submission, the user clicks the 'Return to home' action in the confirmation component.  - Behavior: The page navigates to index.html with no additional URL parameters, as specified in the outgoing navigation configuration. The existing prayer request page state is discarded.

Interaction: Submit another request from confirmation
- Trigger: After a successful submission, the user clicks a 'Submit another request' action.  - Behavior: The page state manager resets the form state to its initial values. It reuses already loaded form options or, depending on implementation, may optionally re-call getPrayerRequestFormOptions to ensure up-to-date options. Confirmation state is cleared so the user sees the entry form again. The URL categoryKey parameter (if present) is re-applied to pre-select the category if it still matches a loaded category key.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load of prayer_request.html, the page reads the URL query parameter categoryKey, if present. This parameter is stored in transient in-memory state and is not written back to the URL. The page then calls getPrimaryNavigation and getPrayerRequestFormOptions. After getPrayerRequestFormOptions resolves, the page compares the stored categoryKey to the keys in the returned categories list; if a match exists, the form's category field is initialized with that key, otherwise the category field is left unselected or set according to implementation rules using only dynamic options. The focus date field is initialized from suggestedFocusDate returned by getPrayerRequestFormOptions, not from any hardcoded value. Privacy options are also initialized only from the privacyOptions array, with an optional default based on the first item in that list. The URL parameter categoryKey affects only the initial category selection; subsequent user changes to the category do not modify the URL. The page maintains an internal view state that can be 'loading', 'form-ready', 'submitting', 'confirmation', or 'error'. These states are managed in memory and are not persisted via localStorage or other client-side storage; they reset on full page reload or navigation away. After a successful submitPrayerRequest call, the state transitions from 'submitting' to 'confirmation', and the response prayerRequest object is stored in memory for display in the confirmation component. Clicking 'Submit another request' returns the state to 'form-ready' and re-applies any existing URL parameter logic for categoryKey. Navigation to index.html from the confirmation state uses a simple location change without additional URL parameters.

components.functionality: On page initialization, fetches primary navigation items using getPrimaryNavigation and renders them in the page header. Each navigation item is displayed using its dynamic label and order, and clicking an item routes the user to the corresponding page as configured by the application router.
event_handlers: onInit (call getPrimaryNavigation and store the result)
onNavigationItemClick (navigate to the route mapped to the clicked item's id)

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading for the prayer request form. Reads the categoryKey URL parameter, calls getPrayerRequestFormOptions, and prepares initial form state including categories, privacy options, suggested focus date, and an optionally pre-selected category if the URL parameter matches one of the returned category keys.
data_binding: PrayerRequest (initial shape only, not yet persisted as a record)
event_handlers: onInit (parse URL for categoryKey, then call getPrayerRequestFormOptions)
onLoadSuccess (store categories, privacyOptions, suggestedFocusDate; compute initial selected category based on categoryKey; notify the form component that data is ready)
onLoadError (populate error-notification component and block form display until resolved)

components.functionality: Manages the focus date input within the prayer request form. It initializes with the suggestedFocusDate from getPrayerRequestFormOptions, allows the user to adjust the date, validates the selected value, and updates the parent form's focusDate state.
event_handlers: onInit (receive initial date from form loader and set as the current value)
onChange (update the form's focusDate state when the user picks a new date)
onValidationError (signal invalid date format or empty value back to the form for validation tracking)

components.functionality: Displays and manages the main prayer request input form. It shows dynamic category and privacy selectors, the focus date picker, message textarea, and optional name and email fields. It maintains field-level and form-level validation state, and upon submission, delegates to the submission-handler component to call submitPrayerRequest.
data_binding: PrayerRequest (category, privacy, focus_date, message, name, email)
event_handlers: onFieldChange (update PrayerRequest field values in state for categoryKey, privacyKey, focusDate, message, name, and email)
onBlur (run per-field validation such as required checks and email format validation, updating error indicators)
onSubmit (run full-form validation and, if valid, trigger submission-handler with the current form state)
onValidationError (prevent submission and display validation messages for any invalid fields)

onOptionsReady (receive a signal from prayer-request-form-loader that categories, privacyOptions, and suggestedFocusDate are available and initialize form fields, including applying URL-based category pre-selection)

components.functionality: Handles the actual call to submitPrayerRequest when the user submits the form. It manages transient states such as 'submitting', processes the response, and informs other components (confirmation and error-notification) of the outcome.
event_handlers: onInvoke (receive validated form data from prayer-request-form and call submitPrayerRequest with categoryKey, privacyKey, focusDate, message, name, and email)
onSuccess (store the response including prayerRequestId, categoryLabel, privacyLabel, focusDate, createdAt, and success message; transition page-state-manager from 'submitting' to 'confirmation')
onError (capture error information and notify the error-notification component; transition page-state-manager back to 'form-ready' while preserving user input)

components.functionality: Displays after a successful prayer request submission. It shows the confirmation message and selected details from the submitPrayerRequest response (such as categoryLabel, privacyLabel, and focusDate) and provides actions to return to the home page or submit another request.
event_handlers: onShow (render confirmation details when page-state-manager switches to 'confirmation')
onClickReturnHome (navigate to index.html with no additional URL parameters as defined by outgoing navigation)

onClickSubmitAnother (signal page-state-manager to reset to 'form-ready', reinitialize the form using existing or freshly loaded getPrayerRequestFormOptions data, and re-apply any URL categoryKey pre-selection)

components.functionality: Displays error messages related to loading form options or submitting the prayer request. It can handle errors from both getPrayerRequestFormOptions and submitPrayerRequest, showing the most relevant message to the user and allowing dismissal.
event_handlers: onErrorRaised (listen for error events from prayer-request-form-loader or submission-handler and update the alert content accordingly)
onDismiss (clear the current error from ErrorState and hide the alert, without modifying form or confirmation data)

components.functionality: Controls overall page state transitions, including loading, form-ready, submitting, confirmation, and error. It coordinates between loader, form, submission-handler, confirmation, and error-notification components, and applies URL parameter logic for categoryKey at the correct time.
event_handlers: onInit (set state to 'loading', read categoryKey from the URL, and trigger prayer-request-form-loader and header-navigation initialization)
onFormOptionsLoaded (switch state from 'loading' to 'form-ready' when getPrayerRequestFormOptions succeeds)
onFormSubmitStart (switch state from 'form-ready' to 'submitting' when submission-handler is invoked)
onFormSubmitSuccess (switch state from 'submitting' to 'confirmation' when submitPrayerRequest returns success=true)
onFormSubmitError (switch state from 'submitting' back to 'form-ready' and raise error-notification)
onResetForNewRequest (after user selects 'Submit another request', reset state to 'form-ready', reapply URL categoryKey-based pre-selection logic, and clear previous confirmation data)

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid typical of WordPress themes, with content max-width likely around 1140–1200px inside full-width sections.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a top-to-bottom linear structure mirroring the mockup: (1) a full-width dark header with logo/brand on the left and navigation on the right, (2) a full-width hero banner with a photographic background and a strong prayer-related headline slightly left-of-center, and (3) a main content section beneath the hero using a two-column layout where the prayer request form occupies the wider left column and supporting functional elements (e.g., alerts, summaries) align with or sit above the right-side column. System-driven states (loader, confirmation, errors) all appear within or over the left main content column without disrupting the global header or hero.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a top-to-bottom linear structure mirroring the mockup: (1) a full-width dark header with logo/brand on the left and navigation on the right, (2) a full-width hero banner with a photographic background and a strong prayer-related headline slightly left-of-center, and (3) a main content section beneath the hero using a two-column layout where the prayer request form occupies the wider left column and supporting functional elements (e.g., alerts, summaries) align with or sit above the right-side column. System-driven states (loader, confirmation, errors) all appear within or over the left main content column without disrupting the global header or hero.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the header occupies roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height, the hero about 55–65%, and the top of the main content (form area and sidebar) begins in the remaining 15–20% that appears just below the fold. Horizontally within the main content container (max-width ~1140–1200px), the left column (form and associated states) uses about 65–70% of the width, while the implied right column (sidebar space for supportive information or future widgets) uses about 30–35%. Components inside the form follow the 8px spacing system, with 24–32px padding inside the form container and 48–64px vertical separation between major content blocks (hero → main content, form → footer, etc.).
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a moderate density: the header and hero are visually bold but contain minimal, focused elements; the main form area is structured and readable with clear groupings, plenty of white space between field groups (16–24px), and a card-like container to prevent the layout from feeling crowded. System messages (errors, loader, confirmation) are visually distinct but compact, stacked near the top of the form column to avoid overloading the user with competing elements.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top-left brand in the dark header, then moves right across the navigation (F-pattern). From there, it drops down to the large white hero headline over the image near the lower-left third of the hero, guided by any accent underline. The flow then continues downward into the left column of the main content where the prayer request form begins. Within the form, attention moves from top (category, privacy, date) downward to message and contact fields, ending on the primary submit button. Secondary attention points are to the top of the form column where alerts and confirmation panels appear, and to the lower-right corner of the viewport where there is visual space reserved for a global floating action button (e.g., chat/WhatsApp) consistent with the original mockup’s secondary focal area.

layout.overall_description: The Prayer Request page opens with a full-width, dark, high-contrast header anchored to the top of the browser window. The church or initiative name sits on the left, feeling solid and institutional, while navigation items are spaced out on the right, all confined within a centered content area about 1140–1200px wide. Directly beneath this, an edge-to-edge hero photograph fills over half the viewport height, evoking community, reflection, or worship. A single, strong headline such as “Share Your Prayer Request” is overlaid slightly left of center, positioned in the lower third of the image in large white type with a subtle accent line underneath to echo the mockup’s editorial feel. As you scroll slightly, the hero gives way to a light main content section. Inside a centered container, the layout splits into two columns: a wider left column that holds a clean, card-like prayer request form with generous padding and structured field groupings, and a narrower right column reserved for supporting content or future widgets, keeping alignment with the original sidebar concept. Loader overlays, error alerts, and confirmation panels appear within the boundaries of the left column, so the global header, hero, and overall grid remain stable. The resulting composition feels modern, calm, and trustworthy, guiding users naturally from brand to hero message to the practical act of submitting their prayer request.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-navigation component forms a full-width horizontal bar fixed at the very top of the page content (not sticky on scroll by default), with a dark background that spans edge-to-edge across the browser window. Within this bar, a centered content container matching the site’s max width (~1140–1200px) is used. The logo/brand text occupies the leftmost 30% of this container, aligned flush to the container’s left edge with about 16–24px padding from the top and bottom, giving it a tall, slightly roomy feel. The navigation menu items are right-aligned within the remaining 70% of the container, displayed as a horizontal row with each item spaced using at least 16–24px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, ensuring they do not feel cramped. On larger screens, the header height visually takes up around 15–20% of the initial viewport height; on smaller screens, it scales down but retains the same proportions and alignment. The header sits directly above the hero section, with no visible gap, creating a strong continuous top frame for the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document flow; full-width background spanning the viewport, constrained content centered within a max-width container. Does not scroll with the user (non-sticky) unless globally overridden by the theme.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The prayer-request-form-loader appears within the main content section and is spatially confined to the left main column that normally contains the prayer-request-form. When active, it overlays this left column with a semi-translucent veil (e.g., 40–60% white or dark tint) that stretches from the top edge of the form column down to its bottom edge, matching its width (about 65–70% of the main content container). At the center of this veiled area, a loading spinner or progress indicator is placed, horizontally and vertically centered within the form column. The right column (sidebar space) and the header/hero above remain visible and unaffected, emphasizing that only the form area is busy. On mobile, where the two columns collapse into a single column, the loader covers the full width of the content area below the hero, still centered vertically within the available viewport height above the fold as much as possible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely positioned overlay within the left main content column (the form area), covering underlying form elements while preserving the overall page layout. It appears and disappears based on loading state controlled by the page-state-manager.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The focus-date-picker is visually integrated as one of the upper fields in the prayer request flow and sits within the left main column, just below or alongside the category and privacy selectors inside the prayer-request-form. On desktop, it is placed in the first or second row of fields, aligned to the left, typically occupying about 50% of the left column’s width when paired horizontally with another field (e.g., privacy level) or the full width when stacked vertically. It maintains a clean, modern input style with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–20px horizontal padding inside the input area, plus 16–24px margin below it to separate it from the next field group. Its label is aligned to the left above the field, with 4–8px spacing between label and input, consistent with the spacing system. On tablets and mobile, the date picker spans 100% of the available width within the column, stacked above the message field and below the category/privacy inputs, ensuring it remains easy to tap and visually prominent in the form’s early flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the form’s content flow, responsive in width: shares a row as a half-width field on larger screens, becomes full-width and stacked on smaller viewports. Its position remains anchored near the top of the form regardless of state, except when the entire form is replaced by the confirmation panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The prayer-request-form occupies the left main column beneath the hero, starting roughly 48–64px below the bottom edge of the hero section to respect the vertical rhythm. Inside the centered main content container (~1140–1200px wide), the form sits flush with the left edge of the left column, taking up about 65–70% of the container’s width. The form itself is presented as a light card or panel with a subtle shadow or border, featuring 24–32px internal padding on all sides, to create a clear, contained area of focus. At the top of this form card, an optional form title (e.g., “Submit a Prayer Request”) sits left-aligned, with 16–24px space below it before the first field group. Fields are arranged in logical vertical groups: category and privacy options appear at the top, followed by the focus-date-picker, then the main message text area (spanning most of the width), and finally optional contact information fields and the submit button at the bottom. Vertical spacing between individual fields is around 16–24px, while spacing between major groups (e.g., message vs. contact info) is closer to 24–32px. The primary submit button is left-aligned at the bottom of the form, with comfortable padding (around 10–14px vertical and 20–24px horizontal) and at least 24px of space above from the last input field. On mobile, this entire left-column form expands to full width, remaining centered with consistent padding relative to the viewport edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left main column, participating in the normal scroll flow. It is conditionally hidden or replaced when the loader or confirmation panel is active, but its spatial footprint on the page (within the left column) remains consistent to avoid layout jumps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Visually, the submission-handler is most evident through the behavior and state of the primary submit button at the bottom of the prayer-request-form in the left column. The button is positioned at the lower-left corner of the form card’s content area, with at least 24px margin to the bottom edge of the card and 24–32px above from the last field to maintain a clear action zone. During submission, the button’s label changes to a loading state (e.g., “Submitting…”) and may show a small inline spinner to the left of the text, keeping the button’s placement fixed so there is no visual jump. Any inline validation messages triggered by the submission-handler appear directly below their respective fields, within the same left column and aligned to the left of the field, using a compact 4–8px vertical gap and high-contrast text color. The button and these messages stay within the card boundaries of the form, reinforcing a tightly associated action area. The right column is not affected and remains visually static during submission.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the form at the bottom of the left column, with state-driven styling changes (disabled, loading) but no change in spatial position. Works in coordination with the form-loader, error-notification, and confirmation panel to transition between states without shifting the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The prayer-request-confirmation panel appears in the same left main column where the form resides, occupying approximately the same footprint to maintain spatial continuity. When a submission succeeds, the form card fades or collapses, and this confirmation panel replaces it directly beneath the hero, still starting about 48–64px below the hero’s bottom edge. The confirmation is presented as a card-like panel with similar 24–32px internal padding, aligned to the left column’s boundaries (65–70% of the container width). Inside, a success icon or subtle graphic is left-aligned or centered horizontally at the top, followed by a clear confirmation heading (e.g., “Your prayer request has been received”) and a brief explanatory paragraph. Text content is set in a comfortable line length within this column, with 16–24px spacing between heading, paragraph, and any follow-up actions (such as a button to submit another request). If there is a secondary action button, it appears beneath the text, left-aligned to mirror the original submit button placement. The right column remains in its original position, giving the impression that only the left column content has changed state, not the entire page layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static replacement of the form within the left column’s space; it does not overlay other sections but conditionally swaps in for the form content as managed by the page-state-manager. It scrolls with the page like normal content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-notification appears as a prominent alert bar within the left main column, anchored at the very top of the prayer-request-form or confirmation area, just below the section heading if present. It spans the full width of the left column (65–70% of the container), matching the card’s inner width, and uses a high-contrast background (e.g., deep red or warm tone) with white or very dark text for visibility. The alert bar has 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, with slightly rounded corners that align with the form’s card style. A small icon may appear on the left side of the bar, followed by the error message text, which is left-aligned. There is about 16px of vertical space below the alert bar before the first form field, ensuring the error feels connected to the form but not crowded. On mobile, the error bar continues to span 100% of the form’s width, maintaining its position at the top of the form or panel so it is seen immediately after the hero when an error occurs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block anchored at the top of the form or confirmation panel within the left column, pushing content below it downward when visible. It does not overlay content and is shown or hidden based on error state controlled by the page-state-manager.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-state-manager does not occupy visible space on the page; instead, it orchestrates which components are visible within the left main column beneath the hero. Conceptually, it controls a stack of states for the area starting roughly 48–64px below the hero and extending down to include the full form column. In the default state, the prayer-request-form is rendered; in loading state, the prayer-request-form-loader overlays this same region; in success state, the prayer-request-confirmation panel replaces the form; and in error state, the error-notification appears at the top of the form or confirmation panel. This manager ensures that the overall page structure—the header, hero, left/right column layout, and vertical spacing between sections—remains stable regardless of which state is active, so users always recognize where they are on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller with no direct spatial footprint. It dictates the conditional rendering and overlay behavior of other components within the left main column while preserving the grid, proportions, and scroll behavior established by the overall layout.

name: My Outreach Plan
title: My Outreach Plan
description: This page lets a user review the outreach events in their personal plan, see key details (date, time, campus, category, activity level), navigate to event details, update simple participation notes for each event, and remove events from their plan.

core_features: Load and display the current user's outreach plan and its items using getMyOutreachPlan.
Show per-event details in the plan list (event title, dates/times, campus, category, activity level, user note) so the user can prepare for upcoming outreach.
Allow users to click an event title to navigate to the event detail page, passing the eventId in the URL.
Allow users to edit and save a simple note on each plan item using updateOutreachPlanItemNote.
Allow users to remove events from their outreach plan using removeOutreachPlanItem, updating the list in place.
Highlight a recently added event in the plan when the page is opened with a highlightEventId URL parameter.
Provide basic feedback (success/error messages) for note updates and item removals.
Display a functional primary navigation bar for site-wide navigation using getPrimaryNavigation.
user_workflows:
View my outreach plan:
1) User navigates to outreach_plan.html (via header, footer, or a direct link from another page).
2) Page calls getPrimaryNavigation to populate the header navigation labels.
3) Page calls getMyOutreachPlan.
4) While loading, the plan content area shows a loading state.
5) On success, the page displays the plan header (title and metadata) and the list of outreach plan items, or an empty state if there are no items.
6) If the highlightEventId URL parameter is present, the page locates the matching event in the items and visually highlights that item.

Review event details from my plan:
1) In the list of outreach plan items, the user scans event titles, dates, and campuses.
2) The user clicks an event title.
3) The page navigates to event_detail.html with a URL parameter eventId set to the clicked item's eventId.
4) The user can later return to outreach_plan.html (potentially with highlightEventId set for that event) to see it highlighted in their plan.

Edit a note for a plan item:
1) User finds an event in the outreach plan list.
2) User clicks an "Edit note" control (or focuses a note field) for that plan item.
3) A note input becomes editable (or an edit form opens) with the current note value from getMyOutreachPlan.items[n].note.
4) User updates the note text and activates the save action.
5) Page calls updateOutreachPlanItemNote with outreachPlanItemId for that row and the new note value.
6) While waiting, the note area for that item indicates a saving state and temporarily disables additional edits.
7) On success, the page updates that item’s note from the response.planItem.note and clears the saving state, showing a success message.
8) On failure, the page restores the previous note value, clears the saving state, and shows an error message.

Remove an event from my outreach plan:
1) User finds an event they no longer plan to attend in the outreach plan list.
2) User clicks a "Remove" control for that item.
3) The page prompts the user to confirm removal.
4) On confirmation, the page calls removeOutreachPlanItem with that item’s outreachPlanItemId.
5) While waiting, the row shows a removing state (e.g., disabled actions) so it cannot be acted on twice.
6) On success, the item is removed from the in-page list; if it was the last item, the empty-plan state appears.
7) On failure, the item remains in the list and an error message is displayed.

Arrive from an event page with a highlighted item:
1) User adds an event to their outreach plan on event_detail.html.
2) After adding, event_detail.html sends the user to outreach_plan.html with the URL parameter highlightEventId set to the eventId that was just added.
3) outreach_plan.html loads getMyOutreachPlan and parses highlightEventId from the URL.
4) After the items load, the page finds the item whose eventId matches highlightEventId.
5) The matching row is visually highlighted and optionally scrolled into view to help the user see what was just added.

Use primary navigation from this page:
1) Page loads and calls getPrimaryNavigation to retrieve navigation items and display them in the header.
2) User clicks a navigation item in the header.
3) Based on the clicked item's id, the site navigates to the appropriate page (URL mapping handled outside this page’s functional definition).
interactions: On page load, call getPrimaryNavigation and render the navigation items in the header.
On page load, call getMyOutreachPlan and render either the plan summary and list of items or an empty state if there are no items.
On page load, read highlightEventId from the URL; once items are loaded, mark the matching event row (by eventId) as highlighted and scroll it into view if possible.
When a user clicks an event title in the outreach plan list, navigate to event_detail.html with eventId set to that item's eventId.
When a user enters edit mode on a plan item note, display the current note value and allow editing.
When a user saves a note edit, call updateOutreachPlanItemNote with outreachPlanItemId and the new note value; then update that item’s note and show success or error feedback.
When a user cancels editing a note (if applicable), revert the note input to its last saved value without calling updateOutreachPlanItemNote.
When a user clicks "Remove" on a plan item and confirms, call removeOutreachPlanItem with outreachPlanItemId; on success, remove that item from the rendered list.
When any interface call fails (getMyOutreachPlan, updateOutreachPlanItemNote, removeOutreachPlanItem), display a non-blocking error message and allow the user to retry actions as appropriate.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by API responses and the highlightEventId URL parameter. On initial load, the page reads highlightEventId from the query string (if present) and stores it in in-page state. The page then calls getMyOutreachPlan. Once the outreach plan items are loaded, the page compares each item's eventId to the highlightEventId. If a match is found, that item is marked as highlighted (e.g., via a boolean flag on that list item in local component state), and the UI scrolls to it if practical. If highlightEventId is missing or no matching item is found, no highlight is applied and the list is rendered normally. Navigation to event_detail.html uses URL parameters only, by setting event_detail.html?eventId=<eventId> when an event title is clicked. This page does not store state in localStorage; all persistent data about the plan and its items (including notes) comes from getMyOutreachPlan and is modified via updateOutreachPlanItemNote and removeOutreachPlanItem. Any feedback messages (success/error) and transient UI states (loading, saving, removing, editing) are managed in memory for the current page load and are not encoded in URL parameters.

components.functionality: Renders the primary site navigation at the top of the page using getPrimaryNavigation. Highlights the current section corresponding to the outreach plan page and allows users to navigate to other main sections of the site.
event_handlers: onNavLoad: Triggered on page load to call getPrimaryNavigation and store the returned navigation items in component state.
onNavItemClick: Reads the clicked navigation item's id and triggers navigation to the corresponding URL (URL mapping handled by the overall site router).

components.functionality: Handles initial loading of the user's outreach plan and items via getMyOutreachPlan, manages loading and error states, and passes the loaded data to child components for display.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Invoked when outreach_plan.html initializes; calls getMyOutreachPlan and sets internal state {loading, error, outreachPlan, items}.
onReloadPlan: Can be invoked when a child component requests a plan refresh (e.g., after a note update or removal if a full reload is desired); re-calls getMyOutreachPlan and updates state.
onLoadSuccess: Sets loading=false, error=null and stores outreachPlan and items into state.
onLoadError: Sets loading=false and error to an appropriate message for display in the UI.

components.functionality: Reads and interprets URL parameters relevant to this page, specifically highlightEventId, and makes them available to other components to influence behavior (such as highlighting a specific plan item).
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parses the current URL's query string for highlightEventId and stores it in page-level state (e.g., pageState.highlightEventId).

onItemsLoaded: After outreach-plan-loader finishes loading items, iterates over items to find the one whose eventId matches highlightEventId; if found, notifies the plan-items-list component to highlight and scroll to that item.

components.functionality: Displays the overall outreach plan information (such as the plan title and optional notes) from getMyOutreachPlan.outreachPlan, along with a simple summary of the items (e.g., count of events). It helps the user understand what this plan represents before scanning the list.
event_handlers: onPlanDataChange: Triggered whenever outreach-plan-loader updates outreachPlan; re-renders the summary area.
onTogglePlanNotesVisibility: Optional handler if the page allows collapsing/expanding any general notes field from the plan (read-only in this context).

components.functionality: Shows a clear, simple message and guidance when getMyOutreachPlan returns no items, indicating that the user has not yet added any events to their outreach plan.
event_handlers: onItemsDataChange: When outreach-plan-loader updates items, this component checks if items.length is zero and conditionally displays the empty state instead of the items list.

components.functionality: Displays the list of outreach plan items returned from getMyOutreachPlan.items. Each item row shows event-specific details (title, date/time, campus name, categoryLabel, activityLevelLabel, user note) and exposes controls to view event details, edit the note, and remove the item. It also applies highlight behavior for the item matching highlightEventId.
event_handlers: onItemsDataChange: Receives the array of items from outreach-plan-loader, stores them in local state, and maps them to rows for rendering.
onHighlightItem: Receives the highlightEventId from route-parameter-handler, finds the row whose eventId matches, sets a highlighted flag on that row, and attempts to scroll it into view.
onItemEventTitleClick: When the user clicks an event title, reads that row's eventId and triggers navigation to event_detail.html?eventId=<eventId>.
onItemEditNoteClick: Puts the selected row into a note-editing state, enabling the note input for editing.
onItemNoteChange: Updates the in-memory draft note value for that row as the user types, without persisting yet.

onItemSaveNoteClick: Validates the draft note if necessary and then dispatches a request to the plan-item-note-editor component (or directly calls updateOutreachPlanItemNote) with outreachPlanItemId and the new note.
onItemCancelNoteEdit: Exits the editing state for the row and restores its note display to the last saved value.

onItemRemoveClick: Initiates a removal flow (likely via confirmation) and delegates to the plan-item-remove-handler component to call removeOutreachPlanItem and, on success, remove the row from the list.
onItemUpdateFromServer: After a successful note update, updates the corresponding row's note and timestamp in the list using the returned planItem from updateOutreachPlanItemNote.
onItemRemovedFromServer: After a successful removeOutreachPlanItem call, removes the corresponding row from the list and, if no items remain, triggers the plan-empty-state component.

components.functionality: Represents a single outreach plan item within the plan-items-list. It displays event information and allows per-item actions: open event detail page, edit and save a note, and remove the item. It also supports a highlighted visual state when its eventId matches highlightEventId.
event_handlers: onEventTitleClick: Reads this row's eventId and initiates navigation to event_detail.html?eventId=<eventId>.
onEditNoteClick: Switches this row into an editing state for the note.
onNoteInputChange: Updates the local draft note value for this row as the user types.

onSaveNoteClick: Calls updateOutreachPlanItemNote with { outreachPlanItemId: thisRow.outreachPlanItemId, note: draftNote }. While the call is pending, disables further note edits for this row and shows a saving indicator.

onSaveNoteSuccess: Receives the updated planItem from updateOutreachPlanItemNote, updates this row's note with planItem.note, clears the editing state, and notifies the feedback-messages component to display a success message.
onSaveNoteError: Restores the previous note value, clears the saving state, and notifies the feedback-messages component to display an error.
onCancelNoteEdit: Exits note-edit mode and restores the last saved note without calling updateOutreachPlanItemNote.
onRemoveClick: Triggers a confirmation prompt; if confirmed, calls removeOutreachPlanItem with thisRow.outreachPlanItemId and sets a removing state on this row.
onRemoveSuccess: Notifies the parent plan-items-list to remove this row from its list and clears any removing state.
onRemoveError: Clears the removing state and notifies the feedback-messages component to show an error message.
onHighlightStateChange: When notified that this row's eventId matches highlightEventId, sets a highlighted flag so that the UI can render it with emphasis.

components.functionality: Encapsulates the logic for editing and saving the note associated with a single outreach plan item, using updateOutreachPlanItemNote. It can be embedded in each plan-item-row.
event_handlers: onStartEdit: Initializes the note input with the current note from the item and enters edit mode.
onInputChange: Updates the draft note value as the user types.
onSubmit: Calls updateOutreachPlanItemNote with outreachPlanItemId and draft note, then emits success or error events back to the parent row.
onSubmitSuccess: Receives response.planItem, updates the parent item’s note, exits edit mode, and triggers a success feedback message.
onSubmitError: Preserves the original note, exits or stays in edit mode depending on design choice, and triggers an error feedback message.
onCancel: Discards draft changes and exits edit mode without calling updateOutreachPlanItemNote.

components.functionality: Centralizes the logic for confirming and executing removal of a plan item from the outreach plan via removeOutreachPlanItem, ensuring consistent behavior and feedback.
event_handlers: onRequestRemove: Invoked by a plan-item-row when the user clicks its remove control; prompts the user for confirmation.
onConfirmRemove: After user confirmation, calls removeOutreachPlanItem with the provided outreachPlanItemId and sets a global or per-row removing state.
onRemoveSuccess: Notifies plan-items-list to remove the item, and triggers a success message via feedback-messages.
onRemoveError: Clears any removing state for that item and triggers an error message via feedback-messages.

components.functionality: Displays transient success and error messages resulting from actions such as updating a note or removing an item. Provides clear feedback without leaving the page.
event_handlers: onNoteUpdateSuccess: Shows a success message when updateOutreachPlanItemNote returns success=true.
onNoteUpdateError: Shows an error message when updateOutreachPlanItemNote fails or returns success=false.
onItemRemoveSuccess: Shows a success message when removeOutreachPlanItem returns success=true.
onItemRemoveError: Shows an error message when removeOutreachPlanItem fails or returns success=false.
onDismissMessage: Allows the user to dismiss a message, clearing it from the UI.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid typical of WordPress themes, with content max-width likely around 1140–1200px inside full-width sections.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear top-to-bottom structure that mirrors the mockup: a full-width dark header navigation at the very top, followed immediately by a full-width hero image band that visually anchors the page. The hero contains the My Outreach Plan title and summary content. Beneath the hero, the page resolves into a centered content area built on a 12-column grid with a dominant left main column for the plan list or empty state and a narrower right sidebar column aligned to existing site patterns (search, WhatsApp, etc.). Within the main column, content flows vertically: summary → list (or empty state) → per-item interactions.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear top-to-bottom structure that mirrors the mockup: a full-width dark header navigation at the very top, followed immediately by a full-width hero image band that visually anchors the page. The hero contains the My Outreach Plan title and summary content. Beneath the hero, the page resolves into a centered content area built on a 12-column grid with a dominant left main column for the plan list or empty state and a narrower right sidebar column aligned to existing site patterns (search, WhatsApp, etc.). Within the main column, content flows vertically: summary → list (or empty state) → per-item interactions.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Above the fold on desktop, allocate roughly 20–25% of the viewport height to the dark header, 50–60% to the hero band with the My Outreach Plan header content overlaid in the lower third, and the remaining 15–25% to the top of the main content and sidebar. Horizontally, inside the max-width container (~1140–1200px), assign about 65–70% of width to the main content column and 30–35% to the right sidebar. Plan item rows span the full width of the main column, with 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px gaps between rows. Major section breaks (hero → main, list → footer) use 48–64px vertical spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderate density: generous white space around major sections and between components, but allow plan item rows to hold multiple data points without feeling cramped. Text blocks should be concise and grouped, with clear separation between each plan item. The hero is visually rich but minimalist (large image, single strong title, compact summary), and the main content area is structured and readable rather than tightly packed. Form controls like the note editor use 8–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding to match the comfortable feel of the header and navigation.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Users start at the top-left logo in the dark header, then scan right across the navigation (F-pattern). Their gaze then drops diagonally down to the hero’s large, left-of-center My Outreach Plan title and summary (Z-pattern), reinforced by a bold accent line or similar element under the title. From there, the eye flows straight down into the main content column, landing on any global feedback messages and then the plan list or empty state. The right sidebar and bottom-right floating actions (like WhatsApp) create a secondary focal area, picking up attention after users have engaged with the primary plan content. Within each plan item row, the eye moves left-to-right: event details on the left, then interactive controls (note editing, remove action) on the right.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a full-width, dark header bar pinned to the top, carrying the church brand name on the left and spaciously spaced navigation links on the right within a centered max-width container. Directly under it, a bold full-width hero image stretches edge-to-edge, occupying over half the visible height on load. Across the lower-left third of this hero, the My Outreach Plan title and a compact summary sit in high-contrast text, grounded by a subtle accent line that echoes the original mockup. Below the hero, the layout tightens into a centered 12-column grid: a wider left main column for the user’s plan content and a narrower right sidebar that visually aligns with the site’s search widget and floating WhatsApp button. In the main column, any temporary feedback messages appear first, followed by either a friendly empty state card or a stacked list of plan item cards with clear separation and comfortable padding. Each plan item reads like a modern card with its title and key event details aligned to the left and light-weight interactive controls grouped on the right. When a note is edited, the card expands vertically in place, preserving the overall rhythm of the list. Throughout, ample vertical spacing between sections and the strong top hero create a composed, editorial feel appropriate for a nonprofit site, while the right sidebar and bottom-right floating button maintain the interactive cues from the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-navigation sits fixed at the very top of the viewport as a full-width dark bar, roughly 72–88px tall (about 20–25% of initial viewport height). Its contents are centered within a max-width container of approximately 1140–1200px. Inside this container, the left 30% of horizontal space is dedicated to the logo and site name, aligned flush left, while the right 70% holds the horizontal navigation links aligned to the right with 24–32px spacing between items. There is 12–16px of vertical padding above and below the nav items to keep them comfortably sized. The header casts a subtle shadow or uses contrast against the hero image below to clearly separate it. On scroll, it remains visible (sticky) while the content beneath moves, maintaining a constant anchor for navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/fixed at top; always full-width; content centered within a max-width container aligned with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The outreach-plan-loader occupies the main content area beneath the hero and within the left main column when data is being fetched. It does not affect the header or hero positions. When active, it overlays or replaces the plan content area starting about 48px below the bottom edge of the hero, spanning the full width of the main column (approximately 65–70% of the page width inside the centered container). Vertically, it shows either a centered spinner or a set of skeleton rows stacking top-to-bottom, each matching the approximate height and width of plan item rows (full width of main column with 16–24px vertical gaps between). There is generous whitespace (at least 32px) above the first skeleton row and below the last, preserving the airy feeling of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally; occupies the same space as the plan list or empty state; does not scroll independently but moves with page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The route-parameter-handler operates entirely behind the scenes and has no visible interface. Conceptually, it is associated with the main content area beneath the hero, interpreting the current route to determine which user’s plan or which filtered view is shown. It does not occupy any space on the grid, create gaps, or shift other components. All layout impact is indirect—controlling which data populates the outreach-plan-header and plan-items-list components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; no spatial footprint; purely logical/stateful behavior tied to routing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The outreach-plan-header is visually integrated into the hero band. It sits over a full-width background image that extends from just below the header-navigation to roughly mid-screen or slightly beyond (about 50–60% of the initial viewport height). Within this hero, the text block is aligned slightly left-of-center horizontally (around 25–35% from the left edge of the page) and positioned in the lower third of the hero’s height to echo the mockup. The block occupies about 40–50% of the hero’s width on desktop, leaving ample negative space on the right. The main title (My Outreach Plan) is large and high-contrast, with a concise one- or two-line summary beneath it. A colored accent line or underline runs beneath the title, roughly 25–40% of the width of the text block, anchoring the headline visually. There is 16–24px spacing between title, accent, and summary text to keep the composition clean. On smaller screens, the text reflows to center or left-align while remaining stacked, and the block uses nearly the full width with generous side padding (16–24px).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits within a full-width hero section; scrolls with the page; overlayed on top of a background image using padding and positioning rather than independent scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-empty-state appears only when the user has no events in their plan and is placed in the main content column directly below the hero and any feedback messages. It is centered horizontally within the main column (which occupies about 65–70% of the page width) and typically spans roughly 60–80% of that column’s width, leaving visible margins on both sides for a light, open feel. The card has generous padding (24–32px on all sides) and at least 48px of vertical space above and below to separate it from the hero and any subsequent content. Inside the card, a simple icon or illustration sits at the top center, followed by a bold heading (e.g., "Your outreach plan is empty"), a short explanatory paragraph, and a primary call-to-action button, all center-aligned. Vertical spacing between these elements follows the 16–24px rhythm. On mobile, the card stretches closer to 100% width with 16px side padding, while maintaining the same vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally in the main content column in place of the plan-items-list; scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-items-list fills the left main content column beneath the hero and any feedback messages whenever the user has events in their plan. It starts approximately 48–64px below the bottom edge of the hero, aligning its left edge with the hero text block’s left margin inside the shared max-width container. The list spans the full width of the main column (about 65–70% of the page width), with individual plan-item-rows stacked vertically. There is 16–24px vertical spacing between rows, and a consistent 24–32px padding at the top and bottom of the list area to visually separate it from adjacent sections. On desktop, the right sidebar begins at the same vertical offset, creating a classic main-content + sidebar layout. On narrower screens, the list expands to 100% width and the sidebar content (if any) stacks below it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static stacked list within the main column; scrolls with the page; its height grows or shrinks based on the number of plan-item-rows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each plan-item-row is a horizontal card occupying the full width of the plan-items-list’s main column. The card has 16–24px of internal padding on all sides and is separated from adjacent cards by 16–24px of vertical gap. Visually, the row is divided into two primary horizontal zones: the left two-thirds (~65–70% of row width) contain the event title in a slightly bolder, larger type, with key details like date, time, campus, and category stacked beneath in one to two lines using a lighter type weight; the right one-third (~30–35% of row width) aligns actions and status. In this right zone, a compact summary of the user’s participation note (or a placeholder like "Add a note") appears nearer the top, with a subtle "Edit note" link or button beside or just below it, and a secondary remove control (icon or text button) anchored toward the top-right or bottom-right of the card. All interactive controls use a minimum 8–12px vertical and 16px horizontal padding to remain touch-friendly. The row background contrasts gently with the page background and may include a subtle border or shadow to reinforce its card nature. On mobile, the layout becomes vertically stacked: event details top, then actions (edit note, remove) aligned below, still with clear spacing and full-width tappable targets.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Repeating, stacked card-like rows in the main list; expands vertically when the related plan-item-note-editor is revealed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-item-note-editor is an inline form fragment associated with a specific plan-item-row. When invoked (e.g., by tapping "Edit note"), it appears directly beneath that row’s main content, visually nested inside the same card boundaries. The card height increases to accommodate the editor, with a clear separation line or extra 8–16px of space between the static event details and the form area. The editor spans nearly the full width of the card’s interior, respecting the card’s 16–24px side padding. It typically includes a multi-line text field and a compact action row for Save/Cancel buttons aligned to the right. The text area uses 8–16px vertical padding internally and occupies enough height (at least 80–120px) to feel comfortable but not overwhelming. The card’s bottom padding increases as needed so that the form elements and buttons have at least 16–24px breathing room from the card edge. On mobile, the editor still sits directly below the item content but stretches to the full width of the card, with buttons stacked or right-aligned within the limited width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and collapses inline within the associated plan-item-row, pushing subsequent rows downward; scrolls with the main content and does not float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-item-remove-handler is presented visually as a small but clear control—either an icon button or a subtle text button like "Remove"—placed within the right-side zone of each plan-item-row. On desktop, it is aligned toward the top-right corner of the card, either on the same horizontal line as the event title’s baseline or just below the note preview/action line, ensuring it’s visually discoverable but not louder than the title. It maintains a minimum tap area of around 32x32px, even if the icon or label itself is smaller, using internal padding consistent with other controls (8–12px vertically, 12–16px horizontally). There is at least 8–16px of separation between this control and the note-edit trigger to avoid accidental taps. On mobile, this remove control may sit on its own line below the note snippet or be aligned to the right while the note edit action is left-aligned, preserving clarity. The control’s visual style (color and weight) is secondary compared to primary actions, matching a quiet but clear destructive or removal style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within each plan-item-row’s right-hand control area; scrolls with the card; no independent positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback-messages component appears as one or more brief notification banners that overlay the content near the top of the main viewport, just below the fixed header-navigation and slightly above the hero or at the top edge of the main content area. On desktop, a single message banner is horizontally centered within the same max-width container as the rest of the content, typically spanning about 50–60% of the page width with 16–24px padding and slightly rounded corners. There is a small vertical gap (8–12px) between stacked messages if multiple are visible. The banner floats above underlying content with a subtle shadow, ensuring it is noticeable but not overpowering the hero. On smaller screens, the banner expands closer to full width with 16px side margins. Each message includes concise text aligned left, optionally with a close icon aligned to the right. When present, plan content (hero and list) remains in place underneath; the message overlays it rather than pushing it down, and dismissing the message simply removes that overlay.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as a fixed or sticky overlay near the top of the content area below the header; appears and disappears without reflowing the main layout; moves with scroll only once it is scrolled out of view if not globally fixed.

name: Contact Us
title: Contact Us
description: Page for visitors to send inquiries to the church, including questions about food pantries, and to view alternative contact information.

core_features: Load and display primary site navigation using the getPrimaryNavigation interface.
Load contact form configuration (subject options and alternative contact info) using getContactFormOptions.
Render a contact form that requires subject, message, and email, with optional name, and supports free-text messages (including pasted phone numbers).
Pre-select the contact subject dropdown based on the optional subjectKey URL parameter when it matches an available subject key.
Validate contact form inputs on submit (required fields present and email format) before calling submitContactMessage.
Submit contact messages using submitContactMessage and display an on-page success or error confirmation using the response.
Provide a "Return to home" navigation control on successful submission that navigates to index.html.
Display alternative contact details (e.g., phone, email, mailing address) using alternativeContactInfo from getContactFormOptions.
