user_workflows: 1) User opens contact.html directly from header/footer navigation. 2) Page calls getPrimaryNavigation to render the main navigation. 3) Page calls getContactFormOptions to retrieve subject options and alternative contact info. 4) User fills in subject, message, name (optional), and email. 5) User submits the form. 6) Page validates the input and calls submitContactMessage. 7) On success, a confirmation panel appears with the response message and summary information. 8) User can click 'Return to home' to navigate to index.html or choose to send another message.

1) User clicks a contextual 'Contact us about this' link from food_pantry_detail.html or other pages, leading to contact.html?subjectKey=<someKey>. 2) On load, the page reads subjectKey from the URL. 3) Page calls getPrimaryNavigation and getContactFormOptions. 4) After getContactFormOptions resolves, if the URL subjectKey matches one of the subjects.key values, that subject is pre-selected in the dropdown; otherwise the dropdown uses the default unselected state. 5) User writes a message (which may include pasted details like a phone number or specific pantry name) and email, then submits. 6) Page calls submitContactMessage with the selected subjectKey, message, name, and email. 7) On success, page shows confirmation with subject label and timestamp from the response. 8) User can choose to return to home or keep the confirmation view.

1) User wants an alternative way to reach the church (e.g., no email access). 2) On page load, getContactFormOptions returns alternativeContactInfo containing phone, email, and mailingAddress values. 3) Page displays these values alongside or below the form. 4) User uses the displayed phone or mailing address outside the web page (e.g., calling or sending physical mail) without submitting the form.
interactions: On page load, trigger an initialization routine that: (a) parses the subjectKey URL parameter if present, (b) calls getPrimaryNavigation, and (c) calls getContactFormOptions.

When getPrimaryNavigation resolves, bind the returned items to a navigation component so that each item label is rendered in the header; the current page (Contact) is visually indicated as active by the consuming application (outside this functional spec).
When getContactFormOptions resolves, bind the subjects array to the subject dropdown options and bind alternativeContactInfo values to the alternative contact component.

If a subjectKey URL parameter exists, after subjects are loaded, attempt to match the parameter value to one of the subjects.key entries and set that as the current dropdown value; if no match is found, leave the dropdown unselected or use the application’s default behavior.
When the user changes the subject dropdown, update the component’s internal selectedSubjectKey state, replacing any pre-selected value from the URL.
When the user types or pastes into the message, name, or email fields, update the respective local state properties without altering any underlying data models until submitContactMessage is called.

On contact form submit, run client-side validation that ensures: subjectKey is set, message is non-empty, and email is non-empty and syntactically valid. If validation fails, prevent the submitContactMessage call and surface inline errors.

If client-side validation passes, call submitContactMessage with the current subjectKey, message, name, and email. During this call, set a submitting state to disable the submit button and prevent duplicate submissions.

When submitContactMessage resolves with success=true, transition the page to a submitted-success state: hide or disable the form (as defined by the application) and show a confirmation panel using the response.message and contactMessage data (subjectLabel and createdAt).

If submitContactMessage resolves with success=false or the call fails, show an error state in the form (e.g., error banner or inline error). Allow the user to modify their inputs and re-submit without losing their existing input.
Within the confirmation panel, when the user clicks the 'Return to home' control, navigate to index.html without additional parameters.

Within the confirmation panel, if a 'Send another message' control is available, clicking it will reset the form fields (subject selection logic may optionally reuse the original URL subjectKey) and clear the confirmation state while keeping the user on contact.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state for this page is driven by the subjectKey URL parameter. On each page load, the application reads subjectKey from the query string (e.g., contact.html?subjectKey=<keyValue>). This value is stored in a transient page-level state (initialSubjectKeyFromUrl). The page then calls getContactFormOptions. Once the subjects array is available, the page attempts to match initialSubjectKeyFromUrl against subjects[i].key. If a match is found, selectedSubjectKey is set to that key; if not, selectedSubjectKey is left undefined or set to the application's default. If the user subsequently changes the subject dropdown, selectedSubjectKey is updated and no longer tied to the URL parameter. The page maintains additional internal states: navLoading/navReady for getPrimaryNavigation, formOptionsLoading/formOptionsReady for getContactFormOptions, formErrors for client-side validation results, and submitStatus (idle, submitting, success, error) for submission progress. When submitStatus transitions to success, the confirmation component becomes active and renders using the submitContactMessage response. Navigating away using 'Return to home' simply changes the page to index.html; no local or persistent storage is used, and URL parameters are the only mechanism for pre-populating the form on future visits.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid typical of WordPress themes, with content max-width likely around 1140–1200px inside full-width sections.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the top-to-bottom sequence from the mockup: (1) a full-width dark header containing the primary-navigation-bar, logo on the left and navigation links on the right; (2) directly beneath it, a full-width hero banner for the Contact Us page with an editorial-style photograph and overlaid page title positioned slightly left-of-center and vertically in the lower third; (3) below the hero, a two-column main content region constrained to a centered max-width (≈1140–1200px), with the contact-form-container occupying the wider left column and the alternative-contact-info-block as a narrower right sidebar; (4) the return-home-link appears near the bottom of the left column, after the form/confirmation content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the top-to-bottom sequence from the mockup: (1) a full-width dark header containing the primary-navigation-bar, logo on the left and navigation links on the right; (2) directly beneath it, a full-width hero banner for the Contact Us page with an editorial-style photograph and overlaid page title positioned slightly left-of-center and vertically in the lower third; (3) below the hero, a two-column main content region constrained to a centered max-width (≈1140–1200px), with the contact-form-container occupying the wider left column and the alternative-contact-info-block as a narrower right sidebar; (4) the return-home-link appears near the bottom of the left column, after the form/confirmation content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, allocate roughly 20–25% of the initial viewport height to the dark header and its padding, 50–60% to the hero image and headline, and allow the remaining space for the main content and sidebar, which then continue below the fold. Horizontally within the main content area, reserve about 65–70% of the width (≈8 of 12 columns) for the contact-form-container and 30–35% (≈4 of 12 columns) for the alternative-contact-info-block sidebar. Within the header, keep roughly 30% width for the brand/logo area and 70% for the navigation. All content sits within a central max-width container inside full-bleed color/image sections.
layout_strategies.content_density: Follow a moderate density: the header and hero remain visually spacious with generous padding and minimal text, while the main content region is more information-dense but kept orderly and breathable. Form controls use 8–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. Group related fields with 16–24px vertical spacing and separate major sections (hero to main content, main content to footer area/return link) with 48–64px to mirror the mockup’s spacious, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the F/Z-pattern from the mockup: eye starts at the logo on the top-left in the header, moves horizontally across the navigation to the top-right, then drops diagonally into the hero’s Contact Us headline in the lower-left region of the image. From there, the eye moves downward into the left-column contact-form-container, scanning from the subject dropdown down through the message fields, ending on the right-aligned submit action. The right sidebar alternative-contact-info-block acts as a secondary focal area, picked up by peripheral vision or after the form. The return-home-link at the bottom-left provides a gentle exit point with low visual weight.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a strong, full-width black header bar pinned to the very top, stretching edge-to-edge across the screen. Inside this bar, content is centered in a wide but bounded layout: the church’s wordmark sits on the far left, and evenly spaced navigation links extend along the right, creating a balanced, modern feel. Directly below, a full-bleed hero image fills most of the remaining initial viewport height, with a photo that feels human and grounded. Over this image, the Contact Us title appears in large, high-contrast type slightly left-of-center and closer to the lower third than the top, with an accent underline echoing the mockup’s editorial style. As you scroll just past the hero’s lower edge, the main content snaps into a clear two-column layout within a centered container: the left side is a broad, white-background column that houses the contact form, while the right side forms a narrower sidebar card with alternative contact details. The form fields are stacked vertically with comfortable spacing, culminating in a right-aligned primary submit button, while the sidebar presents phone, email, and possibly office hours in a tidy vertical list. When a message is successfully sent, a confirmation panel surfaces in the same left column area, keeping the focus where the interaction happened. Near the bottom of the left column, a simple Return to Home link offers a subtle way back to the main site. Overall, the page feels structured and calm: strong top framing, a bold but minimal hero, and a functional, clearly prioritized main area with a supportive sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-navigation-bar sits at the very top of the page as a full-width horizontal strip with a dark (near-black) background. It spans the entire browser width but its inner content is centered within a max-width container of about 1140–1200px. Within this bar, the left-most 30% of the container is reserved for the logo/brand block, aligned flush with the container’s left edge and vertically centered. The remaining 70% is occupied by the navigation links, aligned to the right edge and laid out horizontally with even spacing. The bar’s height is driven by generous vertical padding (approximately 16–24px above and below the nav items), resulting in a header that visually occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the initial viewport. There is no visible border separating it from the hero; instead, the contrast between the dark bar and the bright hero image below defines the transition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the very top of the document flow (optionally can become sticky on scroll, but layout assumes a standard non-overlapping header sitting above the hero).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form-container begins just below the hero section, inside the main content area that is centered on the page. It occupies roughly 65–70% of the available width of the central grid (about 8 out of 12 columns) and is aligned to the left, leaving space for the sidebar to its right. Vertically, there is a 48–64px gap between the bottom edge of the hero and the top edge of this container, providing a clear section break. The container itself reads as a clean, white panel against the page background, with internal padding of about 24–32px on all sides to keep the fields from touching the edges. At its top, it may include a short section title (e.g., “Send us a message”) followed immediately by the contact-subject-dropdown and then the rest of the contact-message-form fields, all stacked in a linear top-to-bottom flow. The form content is aligned to the left, but the container width allows enough horizontal room for certain fields (like name and email) to be arranged side-by-side on larger screens if desired. The overall height adjusts to the number of fields and the message area, typically extending well below the fold on smaller screens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element in the left column of the main content area; reflows to full width above the sidebar on narrow screens (mobile), maintaining its position above alternative contact information.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-subject-dropdown is positioned at the top of the contact-form-container’s internal layout, just beneath any form heading or brief description. It is left-aligned and spans either the full width of the container on smaller screens or approximately half the container width (about 6 out of 12 inner columns) on larger screens if used alongside another field. There is a 16–24px vertical gap above and below it, separating it from the heading and the next set of fields. The dropdown appears as a modern, single-line field with a clear label above it and a subtle border or filled style consistent with the site’s form visual language. Internally, it uses comfortable padding (around 10–14px vertical and 16–20px horizontal) to match the mockup’s moderately spacious input style. Within the form’s vertical flow, it visually anchors the start of interaction, helping users quickly categorize their inquiry (e.g., general question, food pantry, prayer request) before proceeding to the message details below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form control embedded in the top portion of the contact-form-container; it moves with the form as the layout responds, maintaining its position near the top of the field stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-message-form occupies the majority of the vertical space within the contact-form-container, starting directly beneath the contact-subject-dropdown and continuing down to the submit action row. It is laid out as a vertical stack of fields with clear grouping: for example, a row of shorter fields (name, email, phone) arranged in one or two columns on desktop (e.g., 6/6 or 4/4/4 within the container) followed by a full-width message textarea. Each field is separated by approximately 16–24px of vertical spacing, maintaining a clean rhythm. Labels are consistently left-aligned above each field. The message textarea spans the full internal width of the container, visually occupying a more substantial vertical block (3–4 lines by default) to signal its importance. The bottom-most fields align vertically so that the final row of inputs sits just above the contact-form-submission-handler area. Overall, the message form area feels dense enough to be functional but surrounded by enough white space that it doesn’t feel cramped, reflecting the moderate content density of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content area within the contact-form-container, expanding vertically as needed; on smaller screens, any multi-column rows gracefully collapse into single-column stacks while preserving the same top-to-bottom order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form-submission-handler is positioned at the very bottom of the contact-form-container, immediately following the last field of the contact-message-form. It forms a horizontal row that spans the full width of the container but concentrates interactive elements (such as the primary Submit button and any secondary actions like Reset) on the right-hand side, echoing the mockup’s tendency to place key actions toward the lower-right focal area. The primary button is right-aligned, with 16–24px of padding inside the button, and is separated from the preceding field group by about 24px of vertical space to create a clear visual break before the call-to-action. Optional helper text (e.g., “We typically respond within 2–3 business days”) can appear left-aligned within the same row or just above it in a lighter tone. The entire submission row is cushioned by 24–32px of bottom padding inside the container, ensuring the button does not feel cramped against the container’s lower edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored to the bottom of the contact-form-container; as content above expands, it is pushed downward but remains horizontally aligned to the right edge of the inner grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-confirmation-panel occupies the same left-column region as the contact form but appears only after a successful submission. When visible, it is positioned at the top of the contact-form-container’s area, directly under any page-level or section heading, and spans the full width of the left column (the same 65–70% of the content width that the form uses). Visually it reads as a prominent but calm card or banner, with a subtle colored background (e.g., a soft green or light neutral) and generous padding (24–32px) that makes the confirmation message easy to read at a glance. There is 16–24px of vertical spacing between this panel and any remaining form content below. In a typical interaction, the confirmation panel either replaces the form entirely (occupying its former area) or pushes the form down while the form fields fade or collapse, but in all cases the confirmation message stays in the same left-of-center position the user was just focused on. Its height is compact—likely only a few lines of text—but its full-width span and contrasting background give it strong visual presence within the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional, inline block within the left column: hidden by default, then revealed in place above or instead of the form after submission. It does not overlay the rest of the page; instead, it reflows the layout within the contact-form-container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The alternative-contact-info-block sits in the right sidebar of the main content area, aligned so that its top edge is roughly level with the top of the contact-form-container’s inner content (e.g., near the subject dropdown). It occupies about 30–35% of the central content width (approximately 4 of 12 grid columns) and is separated from the left column by a comfortable horizontal gutter (at least 24–32px) to keep the two columns distinct. The block itself appears as a vertical card or panel with a white or slightly tinted background, subtle border or shadow, and inner padding of around 24px on all sides. Inside, contact details—such as phone number, email address, physical address, service times, or pantry info—are stacked in short sections, each separated by 16–24px of vertical space. Headings (like “Call Us,” “Visit Us,” “Food Pantry Info”) are left-aligned and slightly bolder, with details beneath in smaller type. This block extends vertically but generally remains shorter than the full height of the form on the left, increasing its perceived density and functional focus. On longer pages, it may end above the bottom of the form, creating a natural visual taper toward the lower-right area of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar panel in the right column of the main content grid; on smaller screens, it moves below the contact-form-container and expands to full width, maintaining its appearance as a separate card-like block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The return-home-link is positioned near the bottom of the left column, directly beneath the primary interaction area (either under the contact-form-container or, when visible, beneath the contact-confirmation-panel). There is a generous vertical gap—approximately 32–48px—between the bottom of the form/confirmation content and this link, signaling that it is a secondary, post-task navigation option rather than part of the main flow. The link is left-aligned with the rest of the form content, using regular body text size with a subtle styling cue (such as an underline or a small left-pointing arrow icon) to indicate interactivity. It does not occupy a full-width bar; instead it sits as a single line or short line of text on the left, leaving empty space to its right, which preserves the page’s open, minimal feel. On mobile, it remains directly below the main content stack, still aligned to the left margin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline text link at the bottom of the main left column; it scrolls naturally with the page and does not fix or float relative to the viewport.

name: Devotional Sign-up
title: Devotional Sign-up
description: Page for subscribing to email devotionals by selecting frequency, age group, topics, and email format preferences, then submitting contact details to create a devotional subscription.

core_features: Load and display primary site navigation using getPrimaryNavigation for consistent header navigation.
Load available devotional sign-up options (email frequencies, age groups, topics, default frequency) using getDevotionalSignupOptions.
Pre-select email frequency and age group based on incoming URL parameters emailFrequencyKey and ageGroupKey when present and valid.
Provide a devotional subscription form that collects email frequency, age group, topic selections, subscriber name, email address, and a text-only email preference.
Validate required devotional subscription fields (email frequency, age group, at least one topic, email address; name optional per data model) before submission.
Submit the devotional subscription using submitDevotionalSubscription, passing the selected options and contact information.
Display submission success details using the response from submitDevotionalSubscription, including created subscription information.
Display error feedback when loading options or when subscription submission fails, with messages from the interfaces when available.
Offer a navigation action to return to the home page (index.html) after a successful subscription.
user_workflows:
1) Arrive at Devotional Sign-up page:
  - 1.1 The browser navigates to devotionals_signup.html, optionally including emailFrequencyKey and/or ageGroupKey URL parameters.
  - 1.2 On page initialization, the page parses the URL for emailFrequencyKey and ageGroupKey values.
  - 1.3 The page calls getPrimaryNavigation to populate header navigation items.
  - 1.4 The page calls getDevotionalSignupOptions to retrieve available email frequencies, age groups, topics, and a default email frequency key.
  - 1.5 Once options are loaded, initial form state is established:
    - 1.5.1 The selected email frequency is set to the URL emailFrequencyKey if it matches an available option; otherwise it falls back to defaultEmailFrequencyKey from getDevotionalSignupOptions; if that is unavailable or invalid, it falls back to a neutral state (no selection) handled by the form logic.
    - 1.5.2 The selected age group is set to the URL ageGroupKey if it matches an available age group option; otherwise it starts with no selection.
    - 1.5.3 No topics are preselected unless the implementation chooses a neutral state (e.g., all unselected) using only the dynamic options.
    - 1.5.4 The text-only preference is initialized to a neutral default (implementation-defined, not hardcoded around options data).
    - 1.5.5 Subscriber name and email are initially empty.

2) Complete devotional subscription form:
  - 2.1 The user reviews the preselected email frequency and may change it via the email frequency selector, which is populated from getDevotionalSignupOptions.emailFrequencies.
  - 2.2 The user reviews or selects the age group via the age group selector, populated from getDevotionalSignupOptions.ageGroups.
  - 2.3 The user selects one or more devotional topics by checking boxes built from getDevotionalSignupOptions.topics.
  - 2.4 The user optionally toggles the text-only email preference control, which updates the internal textOnly boolean field that will be sent to submitDevotionalSubscription.
  - 2.5 The user enters their name (optional) into the name field.
  - 2.6 The user enters their email address (required) into the email field.
  - 2.7 As the user changes fields, the form may perform inline validation (e.g., email format, required selections) and display validation messages in a validation feedback component.

3) Submit devotional subscription:
  - 3.1 The user activates the form submission control (e.g., clicks the subscribe button).
  - 3.2 The devotional-signup-form component performs client-side validation:
    - 3.2.1 Ensure an email frequency is selected.
    - 3.2.2 Ensure an age group is selected.
    - 3.2.3 Ensure at least one topic is selected.
    - 3.2.4 Ensure a non-empty email address is provided, and optionally that it matches a basic email pattern.
  - 3.3 If validation fails, the form does not call submitDevotionalSubscription and instead:
    - 3.3.1 Populates a list of human-readable validation errors.
    - 3.3.2 Displays these messages in the validation-message-list component tied to the corresponding fields.
  - 3.4 If validation succeeds, the form enters a submitting state:
    - 3.4.1 Disables the submit control to prevent duplicate submissions.
    - 3.4.2 Collects the form values into parameters for submitDevotionalSubscription: emailFrequencyKey, ageGroupKey, topicKeys (array), subscriberName, email, textOnly.
    - 3.4.3 Calls submitDevotionalSubscription with these parameters.

4) Handle subscription submission response:
  - 4.1 On success (submitDevotionalSubscription.success is true):
    - 4.1.1 The page stores the returned subscription object (including devotionalSubscriptionId, emailFrequencyKey, ageGroupKey, topicKeys, createdAt) in its in-memory state for display.
    - 4.1.2 The page transitions from the editable form view to a success state summary view, handled by the subscription-confirmation-panel component.
    - 4.1.3 The confirmation panel displays a success message and may summarize the selected frequency, age group, and topics using the returned subscription data in combination with the previously loaded options.
    - 4.1.4 The confirmation panel shows a "Back to home" navigation control that, when activated, navigates the browser to index.html according to the outgoing navigation definition.
    - 4.1.5 Optionally, the panel may offer a control to create another subscription, which resets the form state and hides the confirmation panel, without modifying URL parameters.
  - 4.2 On failure (submitDevotionalSubscription.success is false or an error occurs):
    - 4.2.1 The page leaves the form in an editable state.
    - 4.2.2 The submission-error-panel displays an error message based on submitDevotionalSubscription.message when available, or a generic error message otherwise.
    - 4.2.3 The submit control is re-enabled so the user can correct input and retry.

5) Use of URL parameters for state and navigation:
  - 5.1 If the user navigates to devotionals_signup.html from index.html with an emailFrequencyKey parameter, the form honors the context (e.g., a user choosing "weekly devotionals" on the home page will see that frequency pre-selected).
  - 5.2 If the user navigates with an ageGroupKey parameter, the age group selector preselects the matching age group, aligning devotional content with the user’s context (e.g., a youth-focused promotion).
  - 5.3 If URL parameters reference keys not present in getDevotionalSignupOptions results, the page safely ignores them and uses the default logic described above.
  - 5.4 The page does not write to or depend on localStorage; all state is kept in memory for the session and inferred again from URL parameters on reload.

6) Return to home workflow after subscription:
  - 6.1 After a successful subscription, the user activates the "Back to home" control in the confirmation panel.
  - 6.2 The page navigates to index.html (without adding new URL parameters) as specified in the outgoing navigation configuration.
  - 6.3 Any existing in-memory form state is discarded when the user leaves the page; if they come back later (e.g., via browser back or a new link), the page re-initializes based on the current URL parameters and API data.
interactions:
Header navigation interaction:
  - On page load, getPrimaryNavigation is called.
  - The header-navigation component renders navigation items using the returned labels and ids.
  - When the user clicks on a navigation item that points to devotionals_signup.html, the browser navigates using standard anchor links and may include emailFrequencyKey and/or ageGroupKey URL parameters depending on the source context (defined on the linking page, not here).

Email frequency selection interaction:
  - The email-frequency-selector component displays options from getDevotionalSignupOptions.emailFrequencies.
  - Changing the selection fires an onChange event that updates the form’s internal emailFrequencyKey field.
  - If the selected option is cleared or changed, dependent validation state for email frequency is updated in real time.

Age group selection interaction:
  - The age-group-selector component displays options from getDevotionalSignupOptions.ageGroups.
  - Selecting an age group triggers an onChange event that updates the form’s ageGroupKey field.
  - Validation state for the age group field updates based on whether a valid selection exists.

Topic checkboxes interaction:
  - The topics-checkbox-group component lists checkboxes using getDevotionalSignupOptions.topics.
  - Each checkbox toggle adds or removes the corresponding topic key from the form’s topicKeys array.
  - The component tracks whether at least one topic is selected and surfaces field-level validation state when no topics are chosen.

Text-only preference interaction:
  - The email-preferences-toggle component represents the textOnly boolean field (e.g., as a checkbox or switch).
  - Toggling it updates the form’s textOnly value and may adjust any descriptive helper text in the UI.
  - This value is passed unchanged to submitDevotionalSubscription when the form is submitted.

Subscriber contact fields interaction:
  - The user types into the subscriber name and email fields.
  - Each keystroke triggers onChange events that update the form’s internal subscriberName and email values.
  - The email field may perform basic format validation on blur or as the user types and provide inline feedback via the validation-message-list component.

Form submission interaction:
  - The user activates the submit control to send their devotional preferences.
  - The devotional-signup-form component performs validation and, if valid, calls submitDevotionalSubscription with: emailFrequencyKey, ageGroupKey, topicKeys, subscriberName, email, textOnly.
  - During the API call, the form is in a submitting state, disabling the submit control and optionally showing a loading indicator.
  - On API response, the form either shows the confirmation panel (success) or the error panel (failure).

Confirmation and navigation interaction:
  - In the success state, the subscription-confirmation-panel shows details of the created subscription using submitDevotionalSubscription.subscription and previously fetched options for labeling.
  - The user can click the "Back to home" control, which triggers navigation to index.html.
  - Optionally, a "Sign up another devotional" control can reset form state and return the user to the editable form, without further API calls until they submit again.

Error handling interaction:
  - If getDevotionalSignupOptions fails, the options-loader shows an error state in a feedback component and may display a retry control that calls getDevotionalSignupOptions again on user request.
  - If submitDevotionalSubscription returns success=false or an error is thrown, the submission-error-panel displays the interface’s message (if provided) and allows the user to adjust inputs and resubmit.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load of devotionals_signup.html, the page parses URL parameters emailFrequencyKey and ageGroupKey from the query string. It then calls getDevotionalSignupOptions to obtain dynamic configuration: emailFrequencies, ageGroups, topics, and defaultEmailFrequencyKey. After successfully loading options, the page initializes form state as follows: (1) emailFrequencyKey: If the emailFrequencyKey URL parameter matches a key in emailFrequencies, it becomes the initial selection; otherwise, if defaultEmailFrequencyKey matches one of the available frequency keys, that becomes the initial selection; if neither is valid, the field starts in an unselected state and must be chosen by the user. (2) ageGroupKey: If the ageGroupKey URL parameter matches a key in ageGroups, it is preselected; otherwise, the age group field starts unselected. (3) topicKeys: initialized as an empty array (no topics selected) so selection is entirely user-driven. (4) subscriberName and email: initialized to empty strings. (5) textOnly: initialized to a neutral boolean default determined by the implementation, without assuming any fixed value from the API. The page maintains an in-memory form state object that mirrors the parameters required by submitDevotionalSubscription and updates this state in response to user interactions (onChange events from selectors, checkboxes, toggles, and input fields). A separate in-memory UI state tracks async statuses: isLoadingOptions, optionsError, isSubmitting, submitError, and hasSubmittedSuccessfully. When the user submits, if validation passes, the form state is sent to submitDevotionalSubscription and the UI transitions to the submitting state. On a successful response, the returned subscription object and message are stored in state, hasSubmittedSuccessfully is set to true, and the confirmation panel becomes visible while the form inputs are locked or hidden. On failure, submitError is populated, isSubmitting is set to false, and the form remains editable. URL parameters are not modified by this page; they only influence initial state. Navigation back to the home page uses a standard link to index.html without encoding any additional state. All state is per-page and per-session; no localStorage or other persistent client storage is used.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getPrimaryNavigation to retrieve the primary navigation items for the site header and exposes them to child navigation components.
event_handlers: onInit
onSuccess
onError

components.functionality: Renders the primary navigation bar using data provided by primary-navigation-loader and allows users to navigate to other sections of the site, including potential links back to this Devotional Sign-up page.
event_handlers: onClick

components.functionality: On page initialization, parses URL parameters (emailFrequencyKey, ageGroupKey), calls getDevotionalSignupOptions, and computes initial form state for email frequency, age group, topics list, and default selections based on the API response and URL parameters.
event_handlers: onInit
onSuccess
onError

components.functionality: Central form component that manages devotional subscription fields (email frequency, age group, topics, subscriber name, email, text-only preference), handles validation, and coordinates submission via submitDevotionalSubscription. It orchestrates child selector and feedback components and controls overall form state.
event_handlers: onChange
onSubmit
onSuccess
onError

components.functionality: Displays the available devotional email frequencies using options from getDevotionalSignupOptions.emailFrequencies and allows the user to choose one. It honors initial selection rules from URL parameters and defaultEmailFrequencyKey and updates the form’s emailFrequencyKey field.
event_handlers: onChange

components.functionality: Displays available age group options from getDevotionalSignupOptions.ageGroups and allows the user to select an age group. It preselects a value based on ageGroupKey from the URL when valid and updates the form’s ageGroupKey field on change.
event_handlers: onChange

components.functionality: Lists devotional topic options from getDevotionalSignupOptions.topics as a set of checkboxes. User selections update the topicKeys array in the form state and the component enforces the requirement that at least one topic be selected.
event_handlers: onChange

components.functionality: Represents the text-only email preference as a toggle control. User interaction updates the textOnly boolean in the form state so it can be sent to submitDevotionalSubscription.
event_handlers: onChange

components.functionality: Contains the input fields for subscriber name (optional) and email address (required). Updates the corresponding subscriberName and email values in form state and triggers validation for the required email field.
event_handlers: onChange
onBlur

components.functionality: Displays client-side validation errors for the devotional-signup-form, mapped to fields such as emailFrequencyKey, ageGroupKey, topicKeys, and email. It updates dynamically when the user edits fields or attempts submission.
event_handlers: onValidationChange

components.functionality: Shows error messages related to failures from submitDevotionalSubscription (e.g., network issues or server-side validation errors). It reads message content from the submitDevotionalSubscription response when available.
event_handlers: onError

components.functionality: Visible after a successful submission. It reads submitDevotionalSubscription.subscription and message to display a success confirmation and optionally summarize the user’s devotional preferences by combining the returned keys with the option labels from getDevotionalSignupOptions.
event_handlers: onShow
onHide

components.functionality: Provides a control in the confirmation state that navigates the user back to index.html when clicked, per the outgoing navigation specification. It is typically only shown when a subscription has been successfully created.
event_handlers: onClick

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid typical of WordPress themes, with content max-width likely around 1140–1200px inside full-width sections.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom hierarchy patterned on the mockup: a full-width dark header at the very top with logo/branding on the left and navigation on the right; directly beneath it a full-width hero section with a photographic background and a devotional sign-up headline slightly left-of-center. Below the hero, the page transitions into a two-column main area constrained to a centered 1140–1200px max-width: the left ~70% houses the devotional sign-up form as a white card, while the right ~30% forms a sidebar column reserved for secondary/supporting content or open whitespace. Within the form, content flows vertically in a logical sequence: global feedback, introductory copy, preference selectors/toggles, contact fields, and submission controls.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom hierarchy patterned on the mockup: a full-width dark header at the very top with logo/branding on the left and navigation on the right; directly beneath it a full-width hero section with a photographic background and a devotional sign-up headline slightly left-of-center. Below the hero, the page transitions into a two-column main area constrained to a centered 1140–1200px max-width: the left ~70% houses the devotional sign-up form as a white card, while the right ~30% forms a sidebar column reserved for secondary/supporting content or open whitespace. Within the form, content flows vertically in a logical sequence: global feedback, introductory copy, preference selectors/toggles, contact fields, and submission controls.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the visible initial viewport roughly allocates 20–25% height to the dark header, 50–60% to the immersive hero with the overlaid sign-up headline, and the remaining 15–25% to the top of the main content/form card and the start of the right sidebar. Horizontally, the header inner content uses about 30% width for the logo/brand block and 70% for the right-aligned navigation links. In the main content container, the form column spans about 8 of 12 grid columns (~65–70%), and the sidebar occupies the remaining 4 columns (~30–35%), maintaining generous left and right gutters. All components respect an 8px spacing system, with section separations around 48–64px and internal group spacing around 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is kept moderately dense but breathable, echoing the minimal, editorial feel of the mockup. The hero carries most of the visual richness through photography while the headline and form card use concise text and clear grouping. Form controls are grouped into clearly separated sections (preferences versus contact details) with 16–24px vertical gaps, and ample padding (24–32px) inside the form card. The sidebar is deliberately lighter in content, acting as visual breathing room and supporting the Z-pattern flow rather than competing with the main form.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye begins at the top-left brand in the dark header, moves horizontally to the right across the navigation, then drops diagonally down into the hero where a bold, high-contrast devotional sign-up headline sits slightly left-of-center over the image. An accent underline or highlight under the headline anchors attention and points downward towards the left side of the main content. From there, the eye flows vertically through the form: global messages at the top, then the email-frequency selector, age-group selector, topics, email preferences, and finally contact fields and the submission area. The right sidebar and bottom-right area remain a secondary focal zone, subtly echoing the mockup’s pattern of a functional element in that region (even if lighter on this page), while confirmation and error states temporarily pull focus by occupying the same primary area as the form.

layout.overall_description: The Devotional Sign-up page opens with a bold, full-width black header bar pinned to the very top of the viewport, containing the church outreach logo on the left and evenly spaced navigation links on the right. Below it stretches a full-width hero image that feels editorial and slightly formal—perhaps a calm, contemplative scene—with a large white headline about devotional sign-ups overlaid slightly left-of-center and positioned around the lower third of the image, accompanied by a subtle colored underline to add emphasis. As you scroll just past the hero’s lower edge, a centered content container (about 1140–1200px wide) reveals a two-column layout: on the left, a clean white card holding the devotional sign-up form begins near the top of this section, while on the right a narrower sidebar column remains mostly open or lightly populated, creating breathing room and visual balance. The form card has generous internal padding and is broken into distinct vertical sections—messages at the top, then preference selectors, then contact details—each separated by comfortable 16–24px gaps. Feedback panels and confirmation states appear in the exact footprint of the form card, ensuring that, at any given moment, the main attention is anchored in the left column under the hero headline. The right column and bottom-right quadrant remain visually quieter, recalling the mockup’s sidebar and floating-action emphasis without overpowering the core task of signing up.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-navigation-loader occupies the exact space of the main header navigation bar at the very top of the page. It spans the full browser width horizontally and has a fixed height similar to the final header (approximately 72–88px tall, including padding). Inside a centered 1140–1200px max-width container, it displays placeholder blocks where the logo and navigation items will appear: a rectangular skeleton block on the far left for the brand mark and several evenly spaced skeleton lines aligned horizontally to the right. The background is a dark grey, echoing the black header style, with a slightly lighter grey for the skeleton elements. This loader sits flush against the top edge of the viewport, with no spacing above it, and pushes all other content downward in the normal document flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the document flow; it is replaced one-to-one by the header-navigation component once data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-navigation component is a full-width horizontal bar anchored at the very top of the page, replacing the loader once ready. It extends edge-to-edge across the viewport with a solid black (or very dark) background. Inside, a centered content container (max-width 1140–1200px) is horizontally divided roughly 30/70: on the left, the church outreach logo and name occupy about one-third of the width, vertically centered within a header height of roughly 72–88px; on the right, the main navigation links are aligned horizontally, evenly spaced, and right-justified, using 16–24px horizontal padding between items to echo the mockup’s airy menu. There is 16–24px of vertical padding inside the bar above and below the nav items. This header sits directly above the hero section with no gap, creating a strong contrast between the dark bar and the image below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the topmost position in the normal flow; it scrolls out of view as the user scrolls down, without sticking or fixing to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The devotional-signup-options-loader appears in the left column of the main content area, exactly where the devotional-signup-form will eventually render. It sits just below the hero section’s bottom edge, with a vertical gap of about 48–64px to maintain the generous inter-section spacing. Horizontally, it is centered within the overall page but constrained to the left 8 of 12 grid columns, giving it about 65–70% of the content width. Visually, it appears as a tall white or light-grey card with subtle shadow, matching the final form card, and contains stacked skeleton lines and rectangular placeholders representing upcoming selectors, checkbox groups, and text fields. There is 24–32px of internal padding on all sides, and 16–24px gaps between skeleton rows to mimic the expected form density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content column; it occupies the form card’s footprint and is replaced in-place by the devotional-signup-form as soon as options data has loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The devotional-signup-form is presented as a prominent white card in the left column of the main content area, directly beneath the hero. The top edge of the card starts roughly 48–64px below the hero’s bottom edge so that it feels visually connected but clearly separated. The card spans about 8 of the 12 grid columns (roughly 65–70% width) and is left-aligned within the centered page container, leaving a distinct right gutter that becomes the sidebar. The card has 24–32px of internal padding on all sides and a subtle drop shadow to separate it from the background. At the top inside the card, an H2-style heading like “Devotional Sign-up” is left-aligned, followed immediately (if present) by the validation-message-list. Below, the form fields are arranged in a linear vertical sequence—email-frequency-selector, age-group-selector, topics-checkbox-group, email-preferences-toggle, and subscriber-contact-fields—with 16–24px of vertical spacing between each logical section. At the bottom of the card, the primary submit button is left-aligned, followed by the back-to-home-link slightly below and to its left. The overall height of the card can extend beyond the initial viewport if needed, encouraging vertical scrolling but keeping the main interactive area anchored beneath the hero’s headline alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the left main column; it scrolls naturally with the page. On successful submission, it is visually replaced by the subscription-confirmation-panel in the same position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The email-frequency-selector is the first interactive preference control within the devotional-signup-form, positioned near the top of the card just below the form heading (and any global validation messages). It is left-aligned and spans the full internal width of the card (minus padding), effectively occupying all 8 grid columns of the form area. A label sits above the control with about 8px of separation, and the control itself uses 10–14px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding if it is a segmented or dropdown-style element, consistent with the mockup’s comfortably sized header/nav items. There is approximately 24px of vertical space beneath this selector before the age-group-selector begins, giving it clear prominence as step one in the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline within the vertical form flow; it does not float or overlay but stretches responsively to match the form card width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The age-group-selector sits directly below the email-frequency-selector within the form, maintaining a consistent left alignment and full-width presence within the card. There is a 16–24px vertical gap above it so it reads as a distinct but related section. Like the frequency control, its label is placed above the input with approximately 8px of separation. The interactive control (e.g., dropdown, radio group, or pill selectors) uses similar padding and typography to the frequency selector, reinforcing the rhythm and hierarchy. The block occupies the same 8-column span as other form elements, creating a clean vertical stack that is easy to scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline in the left column’s form stack; it breaks to full-width on all screen sizes, adjusting only its internal layout for smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topics-checkbox-group occupies a slightly taller section in the mid-portion of the devotional-signup-form. It is placed roughly 16–24px below the age-group-selector and spans the full width of the form card. A descriptive label or short intro line sits at the top of this block, with a small 8px gap above the first row of checkboxes. The checkboxes themselves are arranged in one or two columns depending on viewport width: on desktop, topics may be organized into two columns across the 8 grid columns (each checkbox label taking about 4 columns) to keep the section shorter and visually balanced; on tablet and mobile, they stack into a single column for readability. Vertical spacing of around 8–12px separates each checkbox row. This section visually anchors the middle of the form, providing a denser but still orderly area of choices before the simpler email preference toggles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying a block of the form’s left column; internally, its layout reflows between one and two columns responsively but always remains within the card boundaries.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The email-preferences-toggle sits below the topics-checkbox-group, separated by about 24px of vertical space to mark a shift from content topics to delivery preferences. It is left-aligned and occupies the full width of the form card. A brief label or explanatory line appears above the toggle with about 8px of spacing, and the toggle itself is a compact horizontal control (such as a binary switch or small group of segmented buttons) sized to feel substantial but not dominant—approximately 10–12px vertical and 16–20px horizontal padding within each toggle option. This component visually forms a lighter-weight horizontal accent in an otherwise vertical stack, subtly echoing the mockup’s use of horizontal nav items while remaining subordinate to the main form sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline; it maintains a horizontal arrangement on wider screens and can wrap or shift to a stacked configuration on narrow viewports while preserving its overall position in the form flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subscriber-contact-fields block appears in the lower third of the devotional-signup-form card, following roughly 24px below the email-preferences-toggle. It spans the full width of the card and contains stacked fields such as name and email (and optionally phone or other details), each aligned to the left and stretching across the full 8-column span. Each field has its label positioned above the input with around 6–8px separation, and individual fields are spaced 16–24px apart vertically. Inputs have comfortable padding—8–12px vertically and 16–20px horizontally—mirroring the spacing of other interactive components and contributing to the moderately spacious, modern feel. The last contact field ends roughly 24–32px above the primary submit button to maintain a clear visual break between data entry and form submission.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the form; it forms a continuous vertical group of inputs in the left column and simply grows in height as more fields are added.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The validation-message-list appears at the very top inside the devotional-signup-form card, directly below the form title and above the first input section (typically the email-frequency-selector). It spans the full internal width of the card, matching the 8-column span of the form, and is left-aligned. Visually it takes the form of a lightly shaded panel (e.g., subtle red or amber tint for errors) with 12–16px of padding on all sides and 8–12px of vertical spacing between individual message lines. This block pushes the rest of the form content downward when present but never overlaps other elements. There is about 16px of space between the bottom of this panel and the first field to keep it legible and separate. Positioned high in the left column, it aligns with the main reading flow from the hero headline downward, ensuring that users see validation issues before they re-scan the inputs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears inline within the form card; it collapses entirely when there are no validation messages, allowing the first form field to move up.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-error-panel is placed near the bottom of the devotional-signup-form, directly above the primary submit button and below the subscriber-contact-fields block. It spans the full width of the form card and is left-aligned, occupying the same 8-column area as the other fields. When visible, it appears as a more visually assertive panel than the standard validation list—using a stronger error color and perhaps a small icon—with 12–16px padding and compact but readable text. There is about 16px of space above and below it so it reads as a clear, separate alert tied specifically to the submission event rather than to individual fields. By situating it just above the submission controls, users who press the button and look back down naturally encounter this message in the same region where they acted.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the form card’s lower portion; it expands the vertical height of the form when shown and collapses entirely when not needed, without overlapping other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subscription-confirmation-panel takes over the main form space in the left column after a successful sign-up. It occupies the exact same footprint as the devotional-signup-form card, starting roughly 48–64px below the hero’s bottom edge and spanning about 8 of 12 columns in the centered container. Like the form, it is presented as a white card with 24–32px of internal padding and a subtle shadow. Inside, a prominent success heading is left-aligned at the top, followed by a short confirmation message and any follow-up instructions or calls-to-action. The back-to-home-link, now more central to the post-submission flow, appears near the bottom left of the panel, with sufficient spacing (16–24px) above it so that it reads as a clear next step. The right sidebar column remains structurally in place but visually quiet, keeping all attention on the confirmation content in the left column under the hero headline’s implied vertical axis.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the devotional-signup-form within the left main column; it statically replaces the form when shown and scrolls with the page like any other block-level section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-home-link is a subtle text-based navigation element placed at the lower left within the primary card in focus—either at the bottom of the devotional-signup-form or inside the subscription-confirmation-panel. In the form state, it appears directly beneath the primary submit button, aligned with the left edge of the button, with about 12–16px of vertical spacing to clearly separate it as a secondary action. It is styled as a simple text link or understated button with minimal padding to keep it visually lighter than the primary CTA. In the confirmation state, it may be the key action and thus sits slightly higher in the card, still left-aligned and maintaining similar spacing from the content above. In both cases, it remains within the same 8-column left card area to keep the user’s focus anchored in the main content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element at the bottom of the active card; it does not float or stick, and its vertical position shifts only as content above it grows or shrinks.

name: About
title: About
description: Displays the church’s outreach mission, vision, core values, key outreach programs, and campus information, with navigation into serving, events, and giving pages.

core_features: Load primary navigation items from getPrimaryNavigation and render them in the page header in the correct order.
Load About page content from getAboutPageContent and render mission and vision statements.
Render a list of core outreach values from getAboutPageContent.coreValues.
Render a list of key outreach programs from getAboutPageContent.keyOutreachPrograms, each with a descriptive block and a program-specific CTA.
Implement program CTAs that navigate to serve_opportunities.html or events.html with appropriate URL query parameters based on the outreach program slug.
Render a list of campuses from getAboutPageContent.campuses, including basic location and descriptive information.
Render a giving CTA in the About content that navigates to give.html to support outreach work.
user_workflows: View outreach mission and vision: 1) User navigates to about.html via header, footer, or direct link. 2) Page calls getPrimaryNavigation and getAboutPageContent. 3) After getAboutPageContent resolves, the mission and vision text are displayed in the main content section. 4) User reads mission and vision; no additional input is required.

Explore core outreach values: 1) User is on about.html with content loaded from getAboutPageContent. 2) User scrolls to the core values section. 3) Component iterates over getAboutPageContent.coreValues and displays each value. 4) User may expand or focus individual values if the implementation provides such interactions (purely client-side; no additional API calls).

Navigate into specific outreach programs: 1) User scrolls to the outreach programs section, populated from getAboutPageContent.keyOutreachPrograms. 2) For each program, a CTA control is rendered. 3) When the user clicks a CTA for a program whose slug corresponds to food pantries, the handler navigates to serve_opportunities.html with a URL like serve_opportunities.html?categoryKey=food_pantry. 4) When the user clicks a CTA for a program whose slug corresponds to neighborhood cleanups, the handler navigates to events.html with a URL like events.html?categoryId=neighborhood_cleanups. 5) When the user clicks a CTA for a program whose slug corresponds to workshops and classes, the handler navigates to events.html with a URL like events.html?categoryId=workshops_classes.

Review campus locations and choose where to get involved: 1) User is on about.html with getAboutPageContent.campuses loaded. 2) User scrolls to the campuses section where each campus object is listed. 3) User reads campus descriptions and addresses; optional in-page interactions (such as focusing or expanding a campus row) are handled on the client without new API calls. 4) From this context, user may choose to use other navigation (e.g., header or outreach program CTAs) to find serving or event opportunities at or near a campus.

Support outreach financially: 1) User views about.html and sees a giving-related CTA within the About content area. 2) When the user clicks this CTA, the handler navigates to give.html without additional query parameters. 3) Any subsequent giving flow is handled on give.html using its own interfaces.
interactions: Header navigation click: When a user clicks a primary navigation item rendered from getPrimaryNavigation, the onNavItemClick handler uses the item.id to determine the destination URL (defined by the global routing configuration outside this page) and navigates there.

Outreach program CTA click: When a user clicks the CTA in a program card, the onProgramCTAClick handler inspects the program.slug from getAboutPageContent.keyOutreachPrograms. If the slug represents food pantries, it navigates to serve_opportunities.html with categoryKey=food_pantry in the query string. If the slug represents neighborhood cleanups, it navigates to events.html with categoryId=neighborhood_cleanups. If the slug represents workshops and classes, it navigates to events.html with categoryId=workshops_classes.
Support outreach CTA click: When a user clicks the About-page giving CTA described as 'Support our outreach work', the onSupportOutreachClick handler navigates to give.html with no query parameters.

In-page section navigation (optional): If the implementation provides in-page navigation links (for example, links that scroll to mission, programs, or campuses), clicking these links triggers anchor/scroll behavior only; no new API calls are made and no URL query parameters are added.
Footer About link click: When a user clicks an About link in the footer, the browser navigates to about.html, where getPrimaryNavigation and getAboutPageContent are called again on load.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page does not accept or interpret any URL query parameters; its content is entirely driven by responses from getPrimaryNavigation and getAboutPageContent and by user interactions on the client. The URL for this page is a simple about.html path with no required parameters. All cross-page navigation triggered from this page uses URL query parameters to pass state into other pages: clicking the food pantries outreach CTA navigates to serve_opportunities.html?categoryKey=food_pantry, clicking the neighborhood cleanups outreach CTA navigates to events.html?categoryId=neighborhood_cleanups, and clicking the workshops and classes outreach CTA navigates to events.html?categoryId=workshops_classes. Clicking the support-outreach CTA navigates to give.html with no parameters. No state is stored in localStorage or other client persistence; any state beyond what is encoded in these outgoing URLs is confined to the in-memory state of this page and is reset when the page is reloaded.

components.functionality: Fetches primary navigation items using getPrimaryNavigation on page load and renders them in the header in ascending order of the 'order' field. Each item is clickable and used to navigate to its corresponding site section.
event_handlers: onNavItemClick(navItemId)

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data fetching for the About page by calling getAboutPageContent once when about.html loads. Exposes mission, vision, coreValues, keyOutreachPrograms, and campuses to downstream components once the data is available.
Displays the mission and vision statements for the outreach initiative using the mission and vision fields returned by getAboutPageContent. This section is read-only and has no navigation logic.

Iterates over getAboutPageContent.coreValues and renders each value’s title and description as an item in a list. May optionally allow client-side expand/collapse of longer descriptions without further API calls.
event_handlers: onValueToggle(valueIndex) (optional, client-side only)

components.functionality: Renders the list of key outreach programs from getAboutPageContent.keyOutreachPrograms. For each program, shows its title and description and renders a CTA control. The section determines, based on each program.slug, which target page and query parameters to use when the CTA is clicked.
event_handlers: onProgramCTAClick(programSlug)

components.functionality: Represents an individual outreach program item from getAboutPageContent.keyOutreachPrograms, including its title, description, and a clickable CTA. Delegates navigation behavior to the onProgramCTAClick handler using the program’s slug as input.
event_handlers: onProgramCTAClick(programSlug)

components.functionality: Iterates over getAboutPageContent.campuses and renders each campus’ name, address, and description in a structured list. It is informational only; it does not initiate cross-page navigation on its own.
event_handlers: onCampusRowFocus(campusId) (optional, client-side only)

components.functionality: Displays a single campus from getAboutPageContent.campuses, including its name and address fields. May support client-side interactions such as highlighting a selected campus, but does not call any additional interfaces.
event_handlers: onCampusRowClick(campusId) (optional, client-side only)

components.functionality: Provides a prominent call-to-action within the About content area, described as 'Support our outreach work' in the navigation rules, which routes the user to give.html to support the outreach initiative financially.
event_handlers: onSupportOutreachClick()

components.functionality: Includes an About link that navigates to about.html. While the specific list of footer links may be configured elsewhere, this component ensures that re-entering about.html triggers the same data-loading sequence using getPrimaryNavigation and getAboutPageContent.
event_handlers: onFooterAboutClick()

All of this sits above a full-width footer navigation at the bottom.
